title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces V2B, a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker specifically designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The primary contributions include a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network, which aims to enhance target discrimination by learning dense 3D shape information, and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that performs anchor-free 3D center regression directly from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map. This proposal-free strategy effectively addresses the challenge of unreliable 3D proposals in sparse scenes. The proposed method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on challenging datasets like KITTI and nuScenes, with extensive ablation studies validating the efficacy of its various designs.

**Main Review:**
The submission addresses an important and highly significant problem: object tracking in 3D point clouds, which is crucial for applications such as autonomous vehicles and robotics. The paper is clearly written and well-organized, making it easy to follow the proposed methodology. The content provides sufficient technical details, and the overall submission is technically sound, building upon robust, established deep learning architectures like PointNet++. The motivation behind the various design choices, particularly the shape-aware feature learning and the anchor-free voxel-to-BEV localization, is clear and logically integrated, offering a distinct contribution that differentiates it from prior works like SC3D and P2B. The related work section is comprehensive and adequately positions V2B within the existing literature.

The experimental results are quite impressive, showcasing that V2B significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods by a large margin, particularly in challenging sparse conditions, which is a key strength. Extensive quantitative evaluations on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets strongly support the claims of improved performance. The authors have also conducted thorough ablation studies that validate the contribution of each proposed module, including the template feature embedding, shape-aware learning, and the voxel-to-BEV localization strategy, demonstrating the effectiveness of their chosen approach compared to alternatives like VoteNet.

However, some claims could benefit from further experimental justification and clarification. For instance, while the paper claims to be better at handling sparse point clouds, a comparison between Table 1 and Table 2 reveals that the proposed method suffers a performance drop when switching from all object types to sparse objects, whereas some baselines actually improve. This makes it less clear whether the claimed shape-aware feature truly makes the method more suitable for sparse point clouds in all aspects, warranting further investigation or explanation. Additionally, while the ablation studies are generally thorough, there is no explicit ablation study justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and the shape-aware feature learning module. Furthermore, the ablation study primarily focuses on the 'car' category; expanding this to include all four categories would make the claimed contributions more convincingly generalizable.

A minor point for clarification concerns the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." While the paper states that shape information encoding is only performed during training and discarded during testing to avoid inference time increase, a clearer explanation of how the 'learned dense geometric features' are implicitly leveraged during inference without explicit shape generation would be beneficial for full comprehension. This would also help address concerns about the robustness of this approach to novel or extremely sparse scenarios not encountered during training. The manuscript also contains minor grammatical errors, typos (e.g., "local temple" in Table 3), and awkward phrasings that could be improved with a thorough proofread. Some claims, such as those in lines 378-380 ("stable does not mean accurate"), sound awkward and could be rephrased for better clarity.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately identified the challenges of sparsity in existing 3D SOT methods, which their work successfully addresses. However, the paper could benefit from a more explicit discussion of the limitations of the proposed V2B method itself. For instance, a quantitative analysis of the overall computational cost and real-time performance of V2B, particularly considering the voxelization process and subsequent 3D convolutions, would be crucial for deployment in resource-constrained autonomous systems. Further discussion on the method's robustness to other challenging real-world conditions beyond just point sparsity, such as severe occlusions, varying object scales, sensor noise variations, or dense clutter, would also be beneficial. The paper should also explicitly mention that this proposed method, with its reliance on a BEV map, might not be sufficient for object proposal in indoor scenes.

Regarding societal impact, the paper currently lacks a dedicated discussion. Given its direct application in safety-critical domains like autonomous vehicles and mobile robotics, the positive impacts on safety due to improved tracking are clear and should be explicitly stated. However, a brief acknowledgment of broader ethical considerations, such as potential privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, the critical importance of robustness in preventing accidents due to tracking failures, or potential biases in tracking performance across different object types or environments, would align with best practices for academic publications in this field.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

Summary:
This paper proposes V2B, a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space in sparse point clouds. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The shape-aware network aims to enhance discriminative features by learning dense 3D shape information through template feature embedding and a shape generation module, while the localization network performs anchor-free 3D center regression from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map. The proposed method significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on KITTI and nuScenes datasets, particularly in challenging sparse scenarios.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some significant concerns about the writing quality, technical clarity, novelty, and the depth of the ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods, particularly in challenging sparse point cloud environments, demonstrating strong performance and generalization ability on datasets like KITTI and nuScenes. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, and presents a thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. The proposal-free, dense BEV localization approach offers a valuable alternative to existing methods that rely on 3D proposals, potentially inspiring future research directions. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have several concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. The manuscript has numerous grammatical errors, typos (e.g., "widely in various applications," "local temple" instead of "local template" in Table 3), and awkward phrasings that impede readability. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially concerning is the description of the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." The abstract and introduction state that this network "generates a dense 3D shape to characterize the shape information" and that localization is performed by "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features." However, Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This represents a critical inconsistency that severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually used. A clearer explanation of how the 'learned dense geometric features' are implicitly leveraged during inference without explicit shape generation would be beneficial. Furthermore, the `Lshape` loss weight is set to an extremely small `10^-6`, which seems inconsistent with the claimed significance and observed ablation performance drop, suggesting potential misrepresentation or miscalibration. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful. The initial voxelization step's use of "averaging the 3D coordinates and features" for points in a bin could also be suboptimal, and the method for fusing "previous result's point cloud" for template generation during testing is vague. These issues significantly impact the technical soundness and reproducibility.

Third, while the specific combination and design choices, such as the anchor-free 3D center regression from a dense BEV map, present a valuable approach for sparse data, the underlying components of the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network appear heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection. It is difficult to agree that this is a novel component in itself, and the paper must explicitly cite foundational works such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoders that bear strong resemblance to the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. While the paper effectively addresses the challenge of sparsity, a more comprehensive discussion of the method's own limitations is needed. For instance, there is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material; however, as a tracking problem, this approach likely has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. Further discussion on the method's robustness to extreme occlusion, varying object scales, sensor noise variations, or dense clutter beyond just "sparse point clouds" would be beneficial. Furthermore, a quantitative analysis of the overall computational cost and real-time performance on constrained hardware, especially given that the shape information encoding is discarded during testing, would be crucial for practical deployment considerations.

Regarding societal impact, while the paper's contribution to improving 3D object tracking has clear positive implications for enhancing the safety and reliability of autonomous vehicles and robotics, it currently lacks any explicit discussion. A brief acknowledgment of potential ethical concerns or misuse, such as privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, or the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents due to tracking failures, would align with best practices for academic publications in this field. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces V2B, a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV object tracker specifically designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds for autonomous driving applications. The proposed method comprises a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network, which performs anchor-free 3D center regression from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map. The network architecture is designed to be robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and experimental results on KITTI and nuScenes benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective, significantly improving tracking performance over existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly in sparse conditions.

**Main Review:**

**Strengths:**
The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm, V2B, presents a sensible and novel solution. The specific combination of a shape-aware feature learning network and an anchor-free voxel-to-BEV target localization network is a distinct contribution, effectively addressing the challenge of unreliable 3D proposals in sparse 3D space. Extensive experimental results on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets are impressive, demonstrating that the method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin, especially in challenging sparse conditions. The comprehensive ablation studies further validate the contribution of each module, including the template feature embedding, shape-aware learning, and the voxel-to-BEV localization strategy. The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper (e.g., Figures 2 and 3) are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

**Weaknesses:**
While the empirical results are strong, the technical description suffers from several critical inconsistencies and a lack of precision that impede full comprehension and reproducibility. The most significant flaw lies in the description of the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." The abstract and introduction state that this network "generates a dense 3D shape" and that localization is performed by "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features." However, Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This is a direct contradiction that severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually utilized. Furthermore, the `Lshape` loss weight is set to an extremely small `10^-6`, which seems inconsistent with the claimed significance and observed ablation performance drop, suggesting a potential misrepresentation or miscalibration.

Beyond these technical points, the manuscript contains numerous grammatical errors, typos (e.g., "local temple" instead of "local template" in Table 3), and awkward phrasings that impede readability. Mathematical notation also has minor omissions. The color scheme in Fig. 3 is not explicitly clarified and could be confusing; while helpful, the figure doesn't explicitly clarify the flow of features from the shape-aware module to the BEV localization if the shape generation is discarded.

The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data; however, a more comprehensive discussion of V2B's own limitations and potential failure modes, beyond just data scarcity, would be beneficial. Since this is an object tracking paper, it would be helpful if authors could include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs, as visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but videos are more intuitive. Finally, the paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset (and nuScenes, but parameter tuning guidance is still needed), and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that require parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately identified the challenges of sparsity in existing 3D SOT methods, which their work aims to address. However, the paper could more explicitly articulate the limitations of V2B itself. For instance, a discussion on how the method might perform under other challenging real-world conditions beyond just point sparsity, such as severe occlusions, sensor noise variations, or significantly diverse object shapes not well represented in the training data, would be beneficial. Furthermore, while the shape information encoding is discarded during testing to avoid inference time increase, a quantitative analysis of the overall computational cost and real-time performance on resource-constrained platforms would be crucial for practical deployment considerations.

I also have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However, for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it would be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it. Regarding societal impact, while the positive implications for autonomous driving safety and robotics are clear, a brief acknowledgment of potential negative impacts or ethical concerns is warranted. This could include issues like privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, biases in tracking performance across different object types or environments, or the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents due to tracking failures.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.

reviews.text:
This paper introduces V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker designed for 3D single-object tracking in sparse point clouds. The proposed method adopts a tracking-by-detection framework, implementing a siamese network for template and search area feature extraction. To enhance feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embedded into the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In contrast to previous proposal-based methods, V2B employs a dense detection approach for target localization, directly regressing the 3D center from dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly in sparse scenarios.

While the paper presents a novel combination of techniques and demonstrates strong empirical results, several aspects require significant improvement to fully justify its contributions and ensure clarity and reproducibility.

**Critique and Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Needs Clearer Comparison and Justification of Novelty with Previous Methods:**
    The method proposed is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. While the paper presents a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker and the specific combination and design choices are original, particularly in addressing the challenge of sparse point clouds, the precise differentiation from prior works needs to be more explicitly articulated. The overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature via a Siamese network. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal-based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which experiments show performs much better on objects with sparse points. To further support these claims, I strongly suggest adding qualitative results in addition to Table 5 in the submission to visually demonstrate examples of these improvements, especially for objects with sparse points.

    Furthermore, the claimed novelty of shape-aware feature learning largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). While the shape-aware network builds upon prior shape completion ideas, a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D is crucial. This would justify these modifications and clearly highlight the technical novelty and contribution over existing approaches. Overall, authors should provide a clearer comparison with technically related previous methods, clarifying what is inherited and what is genuinely novel or significantly improved to better solve existing problems.

2.  **Needs Better Presentation and Clarification of the Method Section:**
    While the paper is generally well-written and organized, with informative figures that aid comprehension, the method section, in particular, is hard to follow in places. Tons of technical details are thrown at the readers without providing enough context and motivation for why the architecture is designed as it is. More critically, there are significant inconsistencies and a lack of precision that impede understanding and reproducibility.

    The most significant flaw lies in the description of the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." The abstract and introduction state that this network "generates a dense 3D shape to characterize the shape information" and that localization is performed by "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features." However, Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This is a direct contradiction that severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually used. A clearer explanation of how the 'learned dense geometric features' are implicitly leveraged during inference without explicit shape generation would be beneficial.

    Additionally, the `Lshape` loss weight is set to an extremely small `10^-6`, which appears inconsistent with the claimed significance and observed ablation performance drop, suggesting a potential misrepresentation or miscalibration. The initial voxelization step's use of "averaging the 3D coordinates and features" for points in a bin could also be suboptimal, and the method for fusing "previous result's point cloud" for template generation during testing is vague. These issues significantly impact the technical soundness and reproducibility of the work. The manuscript also contains numerous grammatical errors, typos (e.g., "widely in various applications," "local temple" instead of "local template" in Table 3), and awkward phrasings that impede readability. I would strongly suggest the authors rewrite the method section in the revised submission to address these fundamental inconsistencies and improve clarity.

3.  **More Comprehensive Results and Ablation Studies in the Experiments:**
    While the submission benefits from extensive quantitative experiments on KITTI and nuScenes, showing significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in sparse conditions, there can be more results to help readers understand how the model is working and to fully validate its claims.
    *   As mentioned in point 1, a specific ablation study comparing the proposed shape completion regularization loss against the original SC3D formulation is needed.
    *   Adding qualitative comparisons with both SC3D and P2B across all four categories would be beneficial, especially for the 'cyclist' category, where the proposed method performs much worse compared to SC3D. Visualizations of failure modes would be insightful.
    *   In Table 3, the speed comparison reports P2B at 17 FPS on an NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, whereas the official speed of P2B is 40 FPS on an NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU. This huge gap needs to be explained. A more thorough quantitative analysis of the overall computational cost and real-time performance on constrained hardware would be beneficial for practical deployment considerations, especially considering the voxelization process and subsequent 3D convolutions.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The current submission lacks a dedicated discussion on the limitations of the proposed V2B method and its potential societal impact. While the paper effectively addresses the challenges of sparsity in 3D SOT, a more comprehensive discussion is warranted.

*   **Limitations:** Authors should explicitly discuss scenarios where V2B might underperform, such as robustness to extreme occlusion, varying object scales, or dense clutter beyond just "sparse point clouds." What are the typical failure modes, and how might the reliance on learned dense features (without explicit shape generation during inference) affect robustness to novel or extremely sparse scenarios not encountered during training?
*   **Societal Impact:** The paper's contribution to improving 3D object tracking has clear positive societal implications, particularly for enhancing the safety and reliability of autonomous vehicles and robotics. These benefits should be explicitly stated. However, a brief acknowledgment of potential negative societal impacts or ethical concerns would enhance the work's rigor. This could include discussions on privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents due to tracking failures, or potential biases in tracking performance across different object types or environments.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary:**
This paper proposes V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The method includes a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network, which utilizes global and local template matching and a shape-completion network, and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that performs anchor-free 3D center regression from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map. Extensive evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate that V2B achieves promising results, particularly in challenging sparse scenarios, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

**Main Review:**

The empirical results presented in the paper look promising, showcasing significant performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods on KITTI and nuScenes datasets, especially in sparse point cloud environments. The proposal-free, dense BEV localization approach offers a valuable direction for 3D single object tracking, effectively addressing the challenge of unreliable 3D proposals in sparse scenes. The paper's focus on and validation of performance specifically in sparse scenes is a notable contribution.

However, a major concern arises regarding the novelty of the proposed method. The approach appears to be an ensemble of well-known components, such as template matching (e.g., SiamRPN [1]), shape-completion, and BEV localization (e.g., LaserNet [2]). While the specific combination and adaptation of these techniques for sparse 3D object tracking are acknowledged, the overall originality feels somewhat limited.

Furthermore, the technical description suffers from several critical inconsistencies and a lack of precision, which detracts from the perceived technical soundness and understanding of the novel contributions. The most significant flaw lies in the description of the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." The abstract and introduction state that this network "generates a dense 3D shape" and that localization is performed by "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features." Yet, Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This is a direct contradiction that severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually used. A clearer explanation of how the 'learned dense geometric features' are implicitly leveraged during inference without explicit shape generation is crucial.

Additionally, the extremely small `Lshape` loss weight (set to `10^-6`) seems inconsistent with the claimed significance and observed ablation performance drop, suggesting a potential misrepresentation or miscalibration of its actual contribution. The method for fusing "previous result's point cloud" for template generation during testing is also vague and could benefit from more detailed explanation.

For these reasons, more rigorous ablation studies of the global and local template features are desired to clearly validate their individual contributions and interactions within the proposed architecture. This would help substantiate the claimed effectiveness of these components beyond their empirical performance. The manuscript also contains numerous grammatical errors, typos (e.g., "local temple" instead of "local template" in Table 3), and awkward phrasings that impede readability and clarity, making it difficult for an expert reader to fully comprehend and reproduce the results.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
While the authors adequately identify the challenges of sparsity in existing 3D SOT methods, the paper does not explicitly discuss the limitations of their own proposed V2B method. For instance, a discussion on the overall computational cost and real-time performance of V2B, especially compared to state-of-the-art methods and on resource-constrained platforms, is crucial for practical deployment. Further insights into the method's robustness to extreme occlusion, varying object scales, dense clutter, or specific failure modes beyond just "sparse point clouds" would be beneficial.

Regarding societal impact, the paper currently lacks any discussion. Given its application in autonomous driving and robotics, the positive impacts on safety due to improved tracking are clear. However, a brief acknowledgment of potential ethical concerns or misuse, such as in surveillance applications, privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, or the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents due to tracking failures, would align with best practices for academic publications in this field.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces V2B, a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The proposed method comprises two main components: a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The shape-aware network aims to enhance discriminative features by learning dense 3D shape information of potential targets through template feature embedding and a shape generation module. The target localization network then regresses the 3D center in an anchor-free manner from a dense Bird's Eye View (BEV) feature map, avoiding the pitfalls of low-quality proposals in sparse 3D space. The method is evaluated on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets, demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines, particularly in sparse point cloud scenarios.

**Paper Strengths**

1.  The paper presents a novel combination of techniques for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive and contribute significantly to performance, as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments. The overall Siamese shape-aware feature learning network is a robust approach that builds upon prior shape completion ideas, offering an enhanced method for generating dense geometric features from sparse inputs.
2.  The claims of improved performance are strongly supported by extensive quantitative experiments on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets, showing significant gains over state-of-the-art methods, especially in challenging sparse conditions. Comprehensive ablation studies validate the contribution of each module, including the template feature embedding, shape-aware learning, and the voxel-to-BEV localization strategy. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point clouds intuitively demonstrates the usefulness of the proposed method. The anchor-free, dense BEV localization approach is a distinct contribution that effectively addresses the challenge of unreliable 3D proposals in sparse scenes, offering a valuable alternative to existing methods.

**Paper Weaknesses**

1.  **Clarity and Justification of Key Components:**
    *   **Shape Completion:** It remains vague and, in part, contradictory how the main contributions of the shape completion technique are utilized. The paper claims that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs it for the template. While ablation experiments in Table 4 show a performance drop without shape information, a direct comparison with shape completion on the template as in [13] within the same codebase is needed to definitively assess if the proposed idea is superior. Furthermore, a significant inconsistency arises as the abstract and introduction state that the shape-aware network "generates a dense 3D shape" and that localization uses "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features," yet Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This fundamental contradiction severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually used. The extremely small `Lshape` loss weight (`10^-6`) also appears inconsistent with the claimed significance and observed ablation performance drop, suggesting a potential misrepresentation or miscalibration. Intuitively, it is also unclear how performing shape completion for the template versus the search area makes a difference, or why not both.
    *   **Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization:** The proposed method essentially converts 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max-pooling on the z-axis. It is unclear how feature max-pooling on the z-axis helps the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center. Intuitively, max-pooling often discards information, which would suggest it makes z-axis center estimation harder, contradicting the paper’s claim (line 68) that regression on both 2D and z-axis centers becomes more effective and accurate. A detailed ablation on this voxel-to-BEV component, specifically showing how center estimation (both 2D and z-axis) changes with and without this module, would be beneficial. Additionally, the initial voxelization step's use of "averaging the 3D coordinates and features" for points in a bin could potentially be suboptimal.

2.  **Network Design Justification:** According to the paper (e.g., line 126), PointNet++ is extensively used. It is not justified why more advanced point cloud processing methods are not employed, given that PointNet++ is an older architecture (NeurIPS 2017).

3.  **Evaluation Protocol and Robustness:** While the paper includes evaluations on both KITTI and nuScenes, the robustness of the evaluation, particularly for certain object categories, remains a concern. The evaluation on KITTI, even if following prior work, might still suffer from a limited number of examples for certain object categories (e.g., Van and Cyclist), leading to high variation and potentially less robust conclusions. While nuScenes offers a larger dataset, the depth of evaluation or specific scenarios tested there should also be considered to ensure comprehensive validation across diverse conditions.

**Limitations And Societal Impact**

The authors have adequately identified the challenges of sparsity in existing 3D SOT methods, which their work aims to address. However, the paper does not explicitly discuss the limitations of their own proposed V2B method in sufficient detail. For instance, while the shape-aware feature learning module is discarded during testing to avoid inference time increase, the overall computational cost and real-time performance of V2B should be explicitly stated and compared to SOTA, as this is crucial for deployment in autonomous systems. Further discussion on the method's robustness to extreme occlusion, varying object scales, dense clutter, or novel scenarios not encountered during training would be beneficial. Typical failure modes or scenarios where V2B might underperform could also be insightful.

Regarding societal impact, the paper currently lacks any discussion. Given its application in autonomous driving and mobile robotics, positive impacts on safety due to improved tracking are clear. However, a brief acknowledgment of potential ethical concerns or misuse, such as in surveillance applications, privacy concerns related to tracking individuals, or the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents, would align with best practices for academic publications in this field.

**Rating:** 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
