6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localize and follow objects of interest in sparse point clouds. The proposed method, V2B, consists of a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The shape-aware network is designed to enhance discriminative features by learning dense 3D shape information, while the localization network regresses the 3D center in an anchor-free manner from a dense BEV feature map. The authors claim that this approach achieves new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset and demonstrates strong performance on nuScenes.

While the paper presents a novel combination of techniques to address 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds, with its proposal-free, dense BEV localization approach offering a valuable direction, several fundamental issues prevent its acceptance in its current form.

**1. Clarity and Technical Inconsistencies:**
The paper's clarity is significantly hampered by critical inconsistencies and a lack of precision, particularly concerning the core methodological components. While the overall writing can be followed, the application focus of the proposed technique remains ambiguous. The main claim is that it is a framework for tracking 3D objects, but it is unclear whether it targets single or multiple object tracking. Crucially, the paper lacks detailed explanations of typical tracking components such as track initiation, termination, and occlusion handling, making it appear more akin to a 3D detection method rather than a comprehensive tracking framework.

More critically, the technical description suffers from a fundamental contradiction regarding the "Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network." The abstract and introduction state that this network "generates a dense 3D shape to characterize the shape information" and that localization is performed by "voxelizing the learned dense geometric features." However, Section 3.1.2 explicitly states, "Note that shape information encoding is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing." This direct contradiction severely undermines the understanding of how this key component contributes to inference and if the generated dense shape is actually utilized. Furthermore, the `Lshape` loss weight is set to an extremely small `10^-6`, which seems inconsistent with the claimed significance and the observed ablation performance drop, suggesting a potential misrepresentation or miscalibration. Minor issues like grammatical errors, typos, awkward phrasings, and vague descriptions (e.g., fusing "previous result's point cloud" for template generation) further impede readability and comprehension for an expert reader.

**2. Limited Related Work:**
The related work section and the comparison frameworks appear to be very narrow and cherry-picked. There exist many relevant works in the 2D Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) community (e.g., MOTChallenge) and, more importantly, in 3D MOT that are not discussed or compared against. For 3D MOT, many state-of-the-art methods on KITTI, nuScenes, Waymo, and JRDB datasets are missed. To name just a few:
*   X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene).
*   A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB).
*   A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021 (KITTI, NuScene).
A more comprehensive review and comparison against these established baselines are essential to properly position the proposed work.

**3. Insufficient Contribution and Experimental Validation:**
The paper does not offer significant new theoretical contributions, relying instead on a combination of existing machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While application papers are valued, they necessitate comprehensive experimental evaluation on diverse benchmark datasets and comparison with a broad range of state-of-the-art frameworks to rigorously validate their efficacy.

Despite mentions of evaluations on KITTI and nuScenes, the experimental validation provided is not comprehensive enough. The approach is evaluated using a new metric on KITTI, rather than the widely accepted tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA used by the benchmark. It is also unclear why the approach was not evaluated on the official KITTI test set. Furthermore, the framework's ability to deal with non-rigid objects like humans (and how templates are defined for them) is not explored, and thus it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. The absence of evaluations on other large-scale 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as Waymo further limits the generalizability and robustness claims of the proposed method.

**4. Inadequate Discussion of Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The authors have not adequately addressed the limitations of their own proposed V2B method. While the paper discusses the challenges of sparsity in 3D point clouds, it fails to articulate specific failure cases, the method's robustness to extreme occlusion, varying object scales, or dense clutter beyond just "sparse point clouds." A quantitative analysis of the overall computational cost and real-time performance of V2B, especially compared to state-of-the-art methods, is crucial for deployment in autonomous systems and is currently missing.

Regarding societal impact, the paper currently lacks any discussion. While the application in autonomous driving has clear positive impacts on safety, a brief acknowledgment of potential ethical concerns or misuse, such as in surveillance applications, along with the critical importance of robustness in safety-critical systems to prevent accidents due to tracking failures, would align with best practices for academic publications in this field.

In conclusion, while the paper presents an interesting architectural combination with potentially valuable directions, the fundamental technical inconsistencies, limited related work, and insufficient experimental validation undermine its current contribution. Without resolving these issues, particularly the clarity of the core methodology and the thoroughness of its evaluation, the paper falls short of the standards for acceptance.

**Rating:** 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper theoretically investigates the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms in two distinct scenarios: when test tasks are recurring from the training distribution, and when they are new and unseen. For recurring tasks, the authors propose a novel definition of algorithmic stability, termed (γ, K)-uniform stability, which allows them to derive a generalization bound of O(K/mn) for strongly convex objectives. This bound explicitly captures the dependence on both the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n), offering a more granular analysis than prior O(1/m) results. For unseen tasks, the work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training tasks, demonstrating that this bound is independent of the adaptation batch size K through the use of a coupling technique. The proof techniques leverage established connections between algorithmic stability and generalization bounds.

**Main Review:**

**Originality & Significance:**
The paper presents highly original and significant theoretical results, representing an improvement over existing work. Its primary contribution is the introduction of a novel (γ, K)-uniform stability definition specifically tailored for meta-learning algorithms. This new definition is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task, allowing for a tighter generalization bound of O(K/mn) for MAML under strong convexity, which explicitly incorporates both the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n). This is a significant advancement over previous O(1/m) bounds, providing a more nuanced view of how data scales in meta-learning. Furthermore, the extension of generalization analysis to unseen tasks, leveraging total variation distance and a coupling technique to ensure the bound's independence from the adaptation batch size K, is a valuable and novel contribution within the meta-learning theory landscape. This analysis is particularly important for understanding MAML's transferability across distributions, a core aspect of meta-learning. The work rigorously applies and extends known theoretical techniques to a prominent, widely-used algorithm, advancing the state of the art in theoretical meta-learning.

**Quality:**
While the theoretical claims are ambitious and generally build on sound optimization and statistical learning theory principles, the technical quality of the manuscript suffers from several critical inconsistencies and potential errors that need to be addressed.

First, there is an ambiguity in the definition of 'n' samples per task. The abstract states 'n data points,' the introduction 'n samples,' but footnote 1 clarifies '2n samples per each task.' The problem formulation then explicitly defines two disjoint sets, S_i^in and S_i^out, each of size 'n,' totaling '2n' samples per task. This fundamental inconsistency makes the interpretation of the derived generalization bounds unclear and needs immediate clarification.

Second, a major flaw appears to exist in Proposition 1 concerning the step size schedule. It states 'βt = min(β, µ(t+1)) for ² ≤ 8/µ.' A step size of µ(t+1) would increase with 't,' which is mathematically unsound for convergence in strongly convex optimization; typically, it should be a decreasing sequence. This is likely a typo, but it fundamentally undermines the training error analysis presented.

Third, the condition for βt in Theorem 2 is given as 'βt ≤ 1/(4L+2αρG),' which implies a constant upper bound for the step size. This clashes with the decreasing step size schedule (even if corrected) mentioned in Proposition 1, creating a significant inconsistency in the algorithmic setup across different theoretical results. If a constant step size is used, the training error bound in Proposition 1 would generally be different. These discrepancies cast serious doubt on the correctness and coherence of the theoretical framework and must be resolved.

Aside from these critical issues, the main weakness of the analysis lies in the assumption of the convex loss function (Assumption 1(i)). MAML is often used to learn deep neural networks whose loss is non-convex, therefore, the direct implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain limited. The paper does acknowledge this weakness in Remark 2, which is commendable. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML to 1. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications or challenges when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1. Additionally, while the O(K/mn) bound for recurring tasks is an improvement, the authors acknowledge it may not be universally tight for large K. Assumption 2, stating that 'pi is a non-atomic probability distribution,' might also be restrictive for real-world datasets with discrete features; a brief discussion on this would be beneficial.

**Clarity:**
The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. The introduction effectively sets the context, and the problem formulation is precise. The progression from general definitions to specific theoretical results is logical, and the new stability definition and its implications are explained clearly. However, the paper's clarity is severely hampered by the fundamental inconsistencies mentioned above regarding parameter definitions (especially 'n') and the step size specification across different theoretical results. These discrepancies make it challenging for an expert reader to fully follow and trust the theoretical derivations. There are also a few minor typos:
*   Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
*   Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
*   Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
*   Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
*   Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.
Minor formatting inconsistencies, such as the use of ligatures ('ﬁ') throughout the text, also contribute to a less polished presentation.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately addressed the primary theoretical limitation regarding the strong convexity assumption, acknowledging it as a challenging but important direction for future work. However, it is crucial to emphasize that this assumption significantly limits the direct applicability of these results to modern deep learning architectures. A more explicit discussion on the challenges and potential paths to extend these guarantees to the non-convex setting would be beneficial. The absence of empirical validation is also a limitation for a paper discussing generalization, as it leaves the practical relevance of the theoretical bounds unconfirmed, especially given the strong assumptions.

Regarding societal impact, the paper is a purely theoretical contribution. While it does not introduce new applications or methodologies with direct negative societal implications, a brief statement acknowledging the general positive impact of robust theoretical understanding for responsible AI development would be appropriate. Improved theoretical understanding of generalization can contribute to the development of more robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI systems, which has broad positive societal benefits. Conversely, a lack of generalization guarantees could lead to unreliable systems, so this work helps mitigate such risks. A brief statement on general ethical considerations for meta-learning applications (e.g., bias propagation, fairness across tasks) would also be appropriate for completeness.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Overall Recommendation:** This paper presents a valuable theoretical analysis of MAML-type algorithms, offering novel insights into their generalization properties. While the core contributions are significant and technically sound in principle, several critical inconsistencies and areas requiring further clarification prevent a stronger endorsement. Should these issues be adequately addressed, the paper would be a notable contribution to the field. Therefore, I recommend **Marginal Acceptance (6)**.

**Summary:**
This paper rigorously analyzes the generalization bounds of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms in two distinct scenarios: (1) when the test task is a recurring sample from the training tasks (homogeneous case), and (2) when the test task is an unseen, new task (heterogeneous case). For recurring tasks, the authors introduce a novel definition of algorithmic stability for meta-learning, termed (γ, K)-uniform stability, which allows them to derive an O(1/mn) generalization bound for strongly convex objectives. This bound uniquely captures the roles of both the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n), improving upon prior O(1/m) results. For unseen tasks, the paper establishes a bound dependent on the total variation distance between the new task's distribution and the training task distributions, utilizing a coupling technique to ensure the bound's independence from the adaptation batch size K. The paper is purely theoretical and does not include experimental validation.

**Main Review:**

Overall, I find this to be a good paper with high originality and significant potential impact. The theoretical contributions, particularly the derivation of generalization bounds in terms of data points for the homogeneous case and the total variation-based analysis for the heterogeneous case, are novel and, to my best knowledge, new in the literature. The work is technically rigorous, and the theoretical claims are generally well-supported by detailed analysis, building upon established concepts in optimization and statistical learning theory.

**Strengths (Originality, Significance, Quality, Clarity):**

1.  **Novel Stability Definition and Tighter Bounds:** A key strength of this work is the introduction of a novel (γ, K)-uniform stability definition tailored for meta-learning algorithms. This new definition is a significant advancement as it explicitly accounts for the K-sample adaptation step and allows the authors to derive a tighter generalization bound of O(1/mn) for MAML under strong convexity. This bound is a notable improvement over previous O(1/m) bounds by explicitly incorporating the number of samples per task (n), providing a more nuanced view of how data scales in meta-learning. This refined stability definition offers deeper insights into how both the number of tasks and samples per task contribute to MAML's performance, which is crucial for both theoretical developments and practical insights.

2.  **Generalization to Unseen Tasks:** The extension of generalization analysis to unseen tasks is another valuable and novel contribution within the meta-learning theory landscape. By leveraging total variation distance and a coupling technique, the paper shows that the generalization error depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task, with the bound being independent of the test-time batch size K. This analysis is particularly important for real-world applications of meta-learning, where models often encounter new environments and tasks.

3.  **Technical Rigor and Clarity:** The submission is technically sound and rigorous, with assumptions such as strong convexity and smoothness of the loss functions clearly stated. The problem formulation is precise, and the progression from general definitions to specific theoretical results is logical. The paper effectively distinguishes its contributions from related work and is generally well-written and organized, making it accessible to an expert reader.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**

While the conceptual advancements are highly significant, the technical quality of the manuscript suffers from several critical inconsistencies and areas requiring clarification that need to be addressed before publication:

1.  **Ambiguity in 'n' Definition:** A fundamental inconsistency regarding the definition of 'n' samples per task needs clarification. The abstract and introduction refer to 'n data points' or 'n samples,' but footnote 1 and the problem formulation explicitly define two disjoint sets, S_i^in and S_i^out, each of size 'n,' totaling '2n' samples per task. This ambiguity directly impacts the interpretation and tightness of the O(1/mn) generalization bound and needs to be resolved for the paper's coherence.

2.  **Inconsistencies in Step Size Schedule:** Critical inconsistencies and potential errors were noted regarding the step size schedule. Proposition 1 states 'βt = min(β, µ(t+1)) for ² ≤ 8/µ,' which implies a step size that increases with 't.' This is mathematically unsound for convergence in strongly convex optimization, where step sizes typically need to decrease (e.g., C/(µ(t+1))). This fundamental issue undermines the training error analysis. Furthermore, this clashes with the condition for βt in Theorem 2, which is given as 'βt ≤ 1/(4L+2αρG),' implying a constant upper bound for the step size. These discrepancies cast serious doubt on the correctness and coherence of the theoretical framework and require careful review and rectification.

3.  **Strong Convexity Assumption and Non-Convexity:** The authors state in Remark 2 that strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." While the author response clarified the difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case based on their specific stability notion, this discussion should be explicitly included in the paper to make the rationale clear to readers. While strong convexity is a common assumption in theoretical analyses, it significantly restricts the direct applicability of these results to modern deep learning architectures, which are predominantly non-convex. A more explicit discussion on the challenges and potential paths to extend these guarantees to the non-convex setting, even if it remains future work, would enhance the paper's scope and impact.

4.  **Applicability to Bilevel MAML:** Many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms establish a bilevel structure, which is closer to real-world meta-learning. It would be beneficial to discuss how the results in this paper would adapt or be applicable to the bilevel case, especially given that stability analysis in bilevel programming is an active area of research.

5.  **Tightness of O(K/mn) Bound:** While the O(K/mn) bound for recurring tasks is an improvement by showing n-dependence, it is acknowledged by the authors (and noted by reviewers) that it might not be universally tight for large K due to the K dependence in the numerator. Although the authors mention improving this in Appendix E, the content was not available for review, and this remains a minor gap in the presented results.

6.  **Assumption 2 (Non-atomic Distribution):** Assumption 2, which states that 'pi is a non-atomic probability distribution,' might be restrictive for real-world datasets with discrete features. A brief discussion on the implications or limitations of this assumption would be beneficial.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors adequately acknowledge the primary theoretical limitation regarding the assumption of strongly convex objective functions, and their response clarified the challenges for non-convex settings. This transparency about the current scope is commendable, and I encourage including the discussion into the paper.

Regarding societal impact, the paper is a purely theoretical contribution to understanding the generalization properties of an existing algorithm. It does not introduce new applications, datasets, or methodologies that inherently carry direct negative societal implications. The work aims to improve the theoretical foundations of meta-learning, which can indirectly contribute to the development of more robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI systems. Therefore, the potential negative societal impact is minimal and indirect, related to the broader ethical considerations of AI technology rather than specific to this paper's content. A brief statement acknowledging the general positive impact of robust theoretical understanding for responsible AI development would be a minor beneficial addition.

**Decision:**
The paper presents highly original and significant theoretical contributions to the understanding of MAML's generalization capabilities. However, the identified critical inconsistencies regarding parameter definitions and the step size schedule, along with areas requiring further discussion on the strong convexity assumption and applicability to bilevel settings, need to be rigorously addressed. If these technical issues are resolved and clarifications are provided, the paper will be a valuable addition to the literature.

**Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents a theoretical investigation into the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms, focusing on supervised learning tasks. The authors provide a generalization error bound for strongly convex objective functions in two settings. First, for recurring tasks (where the test task is drawn from the training task distribution), they propose a novel definition of algorithmic stability, termed $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability, which allows for the derivation of an $O(1/mn)$ generalization bound. This bound explicitly captures the roles of both the number of tasks ($m$) and samples per task ($n$). Second, for a more challenging setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks (unseen tasks), they provide a generalization bound that depends on the total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. This bound is achieved using a coupling technique, ensuring independence from the test-time adaptation batch size $K$. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

**Main Review:**

This paper makes an important attempt to study the generalization of MAML, a topic of significant interest in the meta-learning community. The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow, with clear notations, definitions, assumptions, and explanations of the main results. The problem formulation is precise, and the progression from general definitions to specific theoretical results is logical.

**Pros:**
1.  **Importance of Topic and Clarity:** Studying the generalization for MAML-type algorithms is very important for advancing the theoretical understanding of meta-learning. The paper is well-written and easy to follow, with clear notations, definitions, assumptions, and a good discussion of related works.
2.  **Novel Stability Definition and $n$-Dependence:** A key strength is the introduction of a novel $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability definition tailored for meta-learning algorithms. This new definition allows the authors to derive a tighter generalization bound of $O(1/mn)$ for MAML under strong convexity, which is a significant advancement over previous $O(1/m)$ bounds by explicitly incorporating the number of samples per task ($n$). This highlights that the sample number for intra-task training is indeed important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error.
3.  **Analysis for Unseen Tasks:** The extension of generalization analysis to unseen tasks, leveraging total variation distance and a coupling technique to achieve a bound independent of the test-time batch size $K$, is a valuable and novel contribution within the meta-learning theory landscape. This provides a formal framework for understanding MAML's transferability across distributions, a core aspect of meta-learning.

**Cons:**
Despite the conceptual advancements, several significant concerns regarding the technical quality, tightness of bounds, and empirical validation need to be addressed.

1.  **Technical Inconsistencies and Ambiguities:** A major concern lies in several critical inconsistencies and potential errors that undermine the technical rigor of the manuscript:
    *   **Ambiguous Definition of 'n':** The definition of 'n' samples per task is ambiguous and contradictory across the paper. The abstract states 'n data points,' the introduction 'n samples,' but a footnote clarifies '2n samples per each task.' The problem formulation then explicitly defines two disjoint sets, $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$, each of size 'n,' totaling '2n' samples per task. This fundamental inconsistency makes the interpretation of the $O(1/mn)$ bound unclear and impacts the validity of the results.
    *   **Flaw in Step Size Schedule (Proposition 1):** A significant flaw appears in Proposition 1 concerning the step size schedule. It states '$\beta_t = \min(\beta, \mu(t+1))$ for $t \le 8/\mu$.' A step size of $\mu(t+1)$ would increase with 't,' which is mathematically unsound for convergence in strongly convex optimization; typically, it should be a decreasing sequence like $C/(\mu(t+1))$. This is likely a typo, but it fundamentally undermines the training error analysis.
    *   **Inconsistency in Step Size Conditions:** Furthermore, the condition for $\beta_t$ in Theorem 2 is given as '$\beta_t \le 1/(4L+2\alpha\rho G)$,' which implies a constant upper bound for the step size. This clashes with the decreasing step size schedule (even if corrected) mentioned in Proposition 1, creating a significant inconsistency in the algorithmic setup across different theoretical results. If a constant step size is used, the training error bound in Proposition 1 would generally be different. These discrepancies cast serious doubt on the correctness and coherence of the theoretical framework.

2.  **Tightness and Novelty of Bounds:**
    *   While the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training samples) for strongly convex objective functions is generally considered optimal, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not explicitly prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. Specifically, the $O(K/mn)$ bound for recurring tasks (as noted in Alternative Review 2) is not universally tight compared to an $O(1/mn)$ lower bound for small $K$, and this $K$ dependence is a point of concern.
    *   It also feels that the results, while technically derived for MAML, are not entirely surprising in their overall form (i.e., bounds decreasing with total samples), lacking some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or capturing a deeper interplay between meta-training and meta-testing. While the derivations may contain new treatments, the impact on the meta-learning community would be greater if the bounds revealed more unique insights specific to MAML's architecture or training process, similar to findings on support/query split in Chen et al., 2020. The training error characterization mainly follows from existing works (e.g., Fallah et al., 2020), and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. A clearer articulation of the unique challenges when extending these to MAML training would be beneficial.

3.  **Insufficient Empirical Verification:** The empirical verification provided is not sufficient to fully support the theoretical claims and their practical relevance.
    *   **Variations and Seeds:** The curves in the experiments contain large variations, and it is strongly suggested that the authors run more seeds and average the results to make them more convincing.
    *   **Impact of K:** The authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, an experiment to test its impact on generalization is crucial.
    *   **Verification of Total Variation Distance:** The paper provides a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. It would be highly beneficial if the authors could provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric truly captures the generalization error. This would be a significant contribution, potentially offering guidelines for improving meta-learning generalization based on task selection.
    *   **Broader Applications:** Since supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, an experiment in this domain would enhance the paper's applicability and appeal. The absence of empirical validation for a paper discussing generalization is a limitation, as it leaves the practical relevance of the theoretical bounds unconfirmed.

4.  **Assumptions and Scope:** The primary theoretical limitation acknowledged by the authors is the assumption of strongly convex objective functions. While common in theoretical analyses, this significantly restricts the direct applicability of these results to modern deep learning architectures, which are predominantly non-convex. A more explicit discussion on the challenges and potential paths to extend these guarantees to the non-convex setting would be valuable. Additionally, Assumption 2, stating that 'pi is a non-atomic probability distribution,' might be restrictive for real-world datasets with discrete features; a brief discussion on this would be beneficial.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors acknowledge the primary theoretical limitation regarding the strong convexity assumption. Extending these results to non-convex settings is a challenging but important direction for future work. The paper is a purely theoretical contribution and does not introduce new applications or methodologies with direct negative societal implications. However, improving the theoretical understanding of generalization can indirectly contribute to the development of more robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI systems, which has broad positive societal benefits.

**Overall Summary and Recommendation:**
This paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, introducing conceptually novel characterizations like the $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability and the use of total variation distance with maximum coupling for unseen tasks. These ideas are highly significant for advancing the theoretical understanding of MAML. However, the identified technical inconsistencies concerning the definition of 'n' and, crucially, the step size schedules in Proposition 1 and Theorem 2, severely undermine the technical quality and coherence of the theoretical framework. Furthermore, the bounds, while new, do not reveal particularly surprising or exciting insights specific to the composite structure of MAML, and the empirical verification is insufficient to thoroughly support the theoretical claims and their practical implications.

Given the critical technical flaws and inconsistencies, combined with the lack of deeper insights into MAML's unique structure and the limited empirical validation, the paper, in its current form, requires substantial revisions. While the conceptual advancements are notable, the technical foundation needs rigorous correction and clarification before it can be considered a strong contribution. Therefore, I tend to **weakly reject** this paper, but I am open to increasing my score based on the authors’ feedback and a thorough revision addressing these major concerns.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.
reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Summary:
The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

Summary:
The paper presents a theoretical analysis of MAML's generalization properties, introducing a novel (gamma, K)-uniform stability definition tailored for meta-learning algorithms. It characterizes the generalization error for both recurring and unseen tasks, deriving improved bounds that explicitly capture the roles of the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n), and linking unseen task generalization to total variation distance. While these theoretical advancements offer significant insights into MAML's behavior, several critical concerns regarding the tightness of bounds, technical consistency, and practical applicability warrant careful consideration.

Main Review:
The paper offers significant theoretical contributions to the understanding of MAML's generalization capabilities. Its originality lies in introducing a novel (γ, K)-uniform stability definition tailored for meta-learning, which allows for a tighter generalization bound (O(1/mn) or O(K/mn)) compared to prior work, explicitly incorporating the number of samples per task (n). The analysis for unseen tasks, leveraging total variation distance and a coupling technique, is also a valuable and novel contribution within the meta-learning theory landscape. The manuscript is generally well-written and logically organized, with assumptions clearly stated, and the progression from general definitions to specific theoretical results is logical. These results are important for advancing the theoretical understanding of MAML and could be influential for researchers in meta-learning theory.

However, several significant concerns remain, particularly regarding the practical implications and theoretical tightness of the derived bounds, as well as critical technical inconsistencies.

1.  **K-dependence and Bound Tightness**: A major concern, reiterated after the rebuttal, is the behavior of the generalization error with respect to K (the number of shots). Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both suggest that the generalization error increases for a larger K. This theoretical finding conflicts with experimental results typically observed in MAML and other meta-learning papers, raising questions about whether the bound is vacuous. While the authors discuss this in Appendix E, for small K, which is common in meta-learning, the bound appears to be as loose as SGD. Only when K is extremely large (e.g., ~10,000 for 1M total samples), does the error decrease, which is rarely practical in meta-learning. This K dependence in the numerator of the O(K/mn) bound means it is not universally tight compared to the theoretical O(1/mn) lower bound for small K, limiting its practical interpretability and utility for guiding hyperparameter tuning.

2.  **Lack of Empirical Verification**: The theoretical results, such as the generalization error for seen tasks in Theorem 2, are not empirically studied. Providing simple experiments to verify these theoretical predictions, especially the counter-intuitive K dependence, would substantially strengthen the paper and help bridge the gap between theory and practice.

3.  **Technical Inconsistencies**: The technical quality is hampered by several critical inconsistencies that cast doubt on the coherence of the theoretical framework.
    *   The definition of 'n' samples per task is ambiguous and contradictory across the abstract, introduction, and problem formulation (e.g., 'n data points' vs. '2n samples per each task' via two disjoint sets of size 'n'). This fundamental inconsistency makes the interpretation of the O(1/mn) bound unclear.
    *   A more critical flaw appears in Proposition 1 concerning the step size schedule, stating 'βt = min(β, µ(t+1)) for ² ≤ 8/µ.' A step size that increases with 't' is mathematically unsound for convergence in strongly convex optimization; typically, it should be a decreasing sequence (e.g., C/(µ(t+1))). This is likely a typo but fundamentally undermines the training error analysis.
    *   This discrepancy is further compounded by Theorem 2's condition 'βt ≤ 1/(4L+2αρG),' which implies a constant upper bound, clashing with the (even if corrected) decreasing step size of Proposition 1. These fundamental inconsistencies require careful resolution to ensure the technical rigor and correctness of the paper's claims.

4.  **Equation in Line 569**: The equation in line 569 seems not straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So, it is questionable whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are truly the same.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, particularly addressing the K-dependence concerns, and if the above-mentioned fundamental technical inconsistencies can be thoroughly resolved and clarified, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept." However, these issues currently prevent a stronger endorsement.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The primary theoretical limitation, acknowledged by the authors, is the strong assumption of strong convexity and smoothness for the loss functions. This assumption is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks, significantly restricting the direct applicability of these results to the non-convex deep learning models typically used with MAML. While this is a common approach in theoretical work, a more explicit discussion on the challenges and potential paths to extend these guarantees to the non-convex setting would be beneficial. Additionally, Assumption 2, which states that 'pi is a non-atomic probability distribution,' might be restrictive for real-world datasets with discrete features, and a brief discussion on this would be helpful. Regarding societal impact, as a purely theoretical contribution, the paper does not introduce new applications with direct negative societal implications. However, improved theoretical understanding of generalization can contribute positively to the development of more robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI systems, which would have broad societal benefits, though these are indirect.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are clearly stated, societal impact is not mentioned.
