7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary:
The paper addresses the question of zero-shot or fast generalization of value-based deep reinforcement learning agents using visual inputs to image transformation which do not change the (conceptual) state of the environment. The long term goal is therefore training agents which learn efficient, high return control policies both which are robust to irrelevant variations in high dimensional input observations. The ultimate goal seems to be generalization of learned skills to novel environments, but as far as I can tell this particular evaluation philosophy is implemented in experiments.

The paper reviews previous attempts to learn robust state representations to visual variations and formulates clear hypotheses for explaining the causes of difficulties which prior methods faced and were hindered by. The said hypotheses suggest a simplified approach (SVEA) which is empirically developed and evaluated in the remainder of the paper. Control tasks from widely used benchmarks are used to evaluate the method, somewhat less usual is the focus on vision-based high-dimensional versions of such tasks, instead of standard domains such as Atari games and benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari [1], CoinRun/ProcGen [2].
reviews.main_review:
I am generally happy with the paper, I have a few suggestions:

* The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function. Strictly speaking, value estimation is just a crutch. Hence, why bother with robust value estimation? Why not learn value functions without augmentations, use them to discover an optimal policy, and then use supervised learning [3, 4] to encode a robust policy? Please clarify in the text! The proposed method is attempting to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which may not be relevant if all that is needed is good control under diverse observation augmentations. How am I getting this wrong?
* Indeed, for all these environments it is not clear that value prediction from vision is needed at all, since a policy could be derived much faster from full state representations in terms of sample efficiency and computational speed, and then have that policy supervise a vision to action mapping, circumventing the robust value estimation problem altogether.
* There are many possible definitions of generalization in reinforcement learning; one is accurate value prediction in unseen states, as realistic problems have enormous state spaces; another is robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where states may have been seem before but there is extraneous variance in the observations which needs to be dealt with. Finally, there are progressively stronger definitions of generalization, namely across domain gaps which may or may not introduce variability relevant to the control policy itself. Which definition have you tested for in your experiments?
* Could you please clarify in the main text which experiments use some form of state information, e.g. "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, as well as target object information, and which do not? If such information is used, how does it reflect on the ability to extrapolate the presented results to problems where such information is not available.
* Could you please specify for each set of results whether the set of initial states was randomized and by how much? One surefire way to get robustness to high variability in observations is open-loop control, which can also be implemented by non-recurrent architectures, e.g. by relying heavily on proprioceptive inputs or general statistics of observations. 


References:
1. (Natural Atari) Amy Zhang et al. 2018. Natural Environment Benchmarks for Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.06032
2. (CoinRun/ProcGen) Karl Cobbe et al. 2019. Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01588
3. Andrei Rusu et al. 2015. Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06295
4. Wojciech Czarnecki et al. 2019. Distilling Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.02186
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper addresses the question of zero-shot or fast generalization of value-based deep reinforcement learning agents using visual inputs to image transformation which do not change the (conceptual) state of the environment. The long term goal is therefore training agents which learn efficient, high return control policies both which are robust to irrelevant variations in high dimensional input observations. The ultimate goal seems to be generalization of learned skills to novel environments, but as far as I can tell this particular evaluation philosophy is implemented in experiments.

The paper reviews previous attempts to learn robust state representations to visual variations and formulates clear hypotheses for explaining the causes of difficulties which prior methods faced and were hindered by. The said hypotheses suggest a simplified approach (SVEA) which is empirically developed and evaluated in the remainder of the paper. Control tasks from widely used benchmarks are used to evaluate the method, somewhat less usual is the focus on vision-based high-dimensional versions of such tasks, instead of standard domains such as Atari games and benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari [1], CoinRun/ProcGen [2].

Main Review:
I am generally happy with the paper, I have a few suggestions:

* The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function. Strictly speaking, value estimation is just a crutch. Hence, why bother with robust value estimation? Why not learn value functions without augmentations, use them to discover an optimal policy, and then use supervised learning [3, 4] to encode a robust policy? Please clarify in the text! The proposed method is attempting to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which may not be relevant if all that is needed is good control under diverse observation augmentations. How am I getting this wrong?
* Indeed, for all these environments it is not clear that value prediction from vision is needed at all, since a policy could be derived much faster from full state representations in terms of sample efficiency and computational speed, and then have that policy supervise a vision to action mapping, circumventing the robust value estimation problem altogether.
* There are many possible definitions of generalization in reinforcement learning; one is accurate value prediction in unseen states, as realistic problems have enormous state spaces; another is robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations, where states may have been seem before but there is extraneous variance in the observations which needs to be dealt with. Finally, there are progressively stronger definitions of generalization, namely across domain gaps which may or may not introduce variability relevant to the control policy itself. Which definition have you tested for in your experiments?
* Could you please clarify in the main text which experiments use some form of state information, e.g. "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, as well as target object information, and which do not? If such information is used, how does it reflect on the ability to extrapolate the presented results to problems where such information is not available.
* Could you please specify for each set of results whether the set of initial states was randomized and by how much? One surefire way to get robustness to high variability in observations is open-loop control, which can also be implemented by non-recurrent architectures, e.g. by relying heavily on proprioceptive inputs or general statistics of observations. 


References:
1. (Natural Atari) Amy Zhang et al. 2018. Natural Environment Benchmarks for Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1811.06032
2. (CoinRun/ProcGen) Karl Cobbe et al. 2019. Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01588
3. Andrei Rusu et al. 2015. Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.06295
4. Wojciech Czarnecki et al. 2019. Distilling Policy Distillation. https://arxiv.org/abs/1902.02186

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces SVEA (Stabilized Q-Value Estimation under Augmentation), a framework addressing the challenge of stabilizing Deep Q-Learning (DQL) agents with visual inputs when strong data augmentation is applied. The long-term goal is to enable agents to learn efficient, high-return control policies that are robust to irrelevant variations in high-dimensional observations, ultimately aiming for generalization to novel environments. The authors identify two key problems in applying data augmentation to off-policy DQL: high-variance Q-targets stemming from indiscriminate augmentation of successor states for bootstrapping, and over-regularization resulting from Q-value estimation solely based on augmented data.

To mitigate these issues, SVEA proposes a simple yet effective approach that selectively applies augmentation only to the current state's Q-value estimation (not Q-targets), uses a modified Q-objective that jointly optimizes over augmented and unaugmented observations, and optimizes the actor on unaugmented data. The method is empirically evaluated on control tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite and robotic manipulation tasks, focusing on vision-based high-dimensional versions. The work uses both ConvNets and Vision Transformers (ViT), demonstrating that SVEA greatly improves stability, sample efficiency, and achieves competitive or superior generalization compared to state-of-the-art methods. Notably, SVEA's successful application to ViTs in image-based RL without CNNs is highlighted as a significant achievement, underscoring the importance of data augmentation in this context. While the paper reviews previous attempts and formulates clear hypotheses, it deviates from standard benchmarks for generalization such as Natural Atari or CoinRun/ProcGen.

**Main Review:**
The paper presents a valuable contribution by identifying and addressing specific pitfalls of data augmentation in off-policy Deep Q-Learning. The proposed SVEA framework offers an intuitive and simple solution through its principled separation of augmentation for current state and target state Q-value estimation, and its novel data-mixing strategy. This approach is technically sound in its core ideas, and the claims are generally supported by extensive empirical evaluations, including strong baselines and crucial ablation studies demonstrating SVEA's superior stability, sample efficiency, and competitive generalization across DeepMind Control Suite and robotic manipulation tasks, using both ConvNets and ViT architectures. The clarity and organization of the paper are commendable, with effective problem articulation and informative figures.

However, despite these strengths, several aspects require further clarification and refinement to fully substantiate the claims and enhance the paper's rigor:

*   **Purpose of Robust Value Estimation vs. Policy Learning:** The goal of reinforcement learning is discovering an optimal control policy, not necessarily an action-value function; value estimation often serves as a means to that end. It is not entirely clear why robust value estimation is strictly necessary for the stated long-term goal. The paper should clarify why robust value estimation is preferred over alternative approaches, such as learning policies without augmentations and then using supervised learning techniques (e.g., policy distillation [3, 4]) to encode a robust policy. This distinction is crucial as the proposed method attempts to solve a problem (RL under observation augmentation) which might be circumvented if the ultimate need is good control under diverse observation augmentations.
*   **Necessity of Vision-based Value Prediction:** For the environments considered, it is not explicitly demonstrated that value prediction from vision is inherently needed. A policy could potentially be derived much faster and with greater sample efficiency from full state representations, and then used to supervise a vision-to-action mapping, thereby bypassing the robust value estimation problem altogether. A discussion on this point would strengthen the motivation for the chosen approach.
*   **Definition of Generalization:** The paper discusses generalization, but there are many possible definitions in RL. These include accurate value prediction in unseen states, robust value prediction from high-dimensional observations with extraneous variance, or generalization across domain gaps. The paper needs to explicitly clarify which specific definition of generalization is being tested and addressed in the experiments, as this directly impacts the interpretation of the results.
*   **Use of State Information:** Please clarify in the main text which experiments, if any, utilize some form of direct state information (e.g., "proprioceptive" data common in control tasks, or target object information), and which rely purely on visual inputs. If such information is used, its impact on the generalizability of the results to problems where only high-dimensional visual observations are available needs to be discussed.
*   **Randomization of Initial States:** For each set of results, it would be beneficial to specify whether the set of initial states was randomized and to what extent. Robustness to high variability in observations can sometimes be achieved by open-loop control or heavy reliance on proprioceptive inputs, so clarifying the randomization strategy provides important context.

Furthermore, while the high-level ideas are sound and the empirical results compelling, the technical presentation exhibits several inconsistencies that detract from the paper's overall quality and rigor:
*   **Equation (2) and Notation:** Equation (2), defining the target parameter update, appears to be missing a parameter symbol (e.g., 'theta') on the right-hand side, making it incomplete. Similarly, Equation (7) uses `k.k_2` for an L2 norm, but the accompanying text and notation `ht = [qtgt_t]N` and `gi = [si, saug_i]N` suggest batch operations where `ht` should be a vector of targets, not a single scalar `qtgt_t`. These inconsistencies hinder precise understanding.
*   **Definition 1 (Optimality-Invariant State Transformation):** The definition states `Q(s, a) = Q(τ(s,ν), a)`, implying an exact equivalence. This is a very strong and likely impractical assumption for real-world augmentations. A more nuanced definition acknowledging approximation or conditions under which such invariance holds would be more appropriate and realistic.
*   **Description of Equation (3):** The description of Equation (3) as a 'nonnegative linear combination of LQ over two individual data streams' is imprecise; it is a sum of two distinct loss terms.
*   **Algorithm 1 Pseudocode:** Lines 8-9 in Algorithm 1 imply element-wise sampling and augmentation within a batch loop. This is computationally inefficient and misrepresents typical batched implementations in deep learning, where operations are vectorized.
*   **Strong Claims Requiring Substantiation:** The claim of being 'the first to successfully solve image-based RL tasks without CNNs' is extremely strong and requires more rigorous substantiation, including a comprehensive survey of existing literature on Transformers in RL to avoid overstatement. While the application of ViTs with SVEA is a noteworthy contribution, the absolute "first" claim needs careful qualification.
*   **Clarity of Quantitative Claims:** While figures are informative, the precise details supporting specific quantitative claims, such as the '6x wall-time' claim in Figure 6 (left), are not explicitly provided in the main text, which would enhance transparency. Minor grammatical errors, such as 'in fact decrease sample efficiency' (should be 'decreases'), and small notational inconsistencies (e.g., `maxa0t Qtgt (st+1, a0t)` in text vs. `maxa0t Qtgt (st+1, a0t)` in Equation (1)) also impact precision.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors include a "Broader Impact" section, which is commendable for acknowledging the "growing ecological and economical footprint of deep learning – and RL in particular – with increasingly large models such as ViT." However, this discussion is quite brief and generic. It would benefit from a deeper exploration of specific negative societal impacts that might arise from this work, such as potential biases introduced or amplified by specific data augmentation choices, or the ethical considerations of deploying highly generalizable RL agents in sensitive real-world applications. The discussion on the ecological and economical footprint could also be expanded slightly to acknowledge that these methods, despite their improvements, might still be inaccessible for researchers with limited computational resources or for deployment in highly constrained environments.

Furthermore, the paper's aspirational goals, such as "generalization to novel environments" and "great improvements in stability and sample efficiency," are stated in the abstract as if fully achieved. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well. It is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many orders of magnitude "greatly improved sample efficiency" entails, or how augmentations themselves worsen the problem. The paper needs to quantify the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms more explicitly. For example, while SVEA improves computational efficiency compared to some other generalization methods, the absolute resource requirements for training large ViT models with extensive data augmentation still remain substantial. Additionally, while SVEA stabilizes data augmentation, the optimal choice of augmentation policies itself remains a challenging aspect, and the paper implicitly acknowledges this. Explicitly mentioning this as a remaining area for future research or a practical limitation for users would be constructive. A discussion on the sensitivity of SVEA to its coefficients (e.g., `alpha=beta=0.5`) or the types of augmentations it might still struggle with would also be beneficial, along with a broader discussion on the inherent limitations of data augmentation for generalization (e.g., it doesn't address shifts in dynamics or task objectives).

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

I understand why every paper claims the maximal general applicability possible which cannot be refuted by reviewers without experiments; please note that proving negatives is hard, and much rests on your honesty and expertise alone. I am not comfortable with text which talks about aspirational goals as if they were achieved in this paper. The abstract talks about generalization to novel environments and great improvements in stability and sample efficiency, it seems to me that not enough attention to detail has been expended in quantifying the limitations of this approach and the wider family of algorithms to which it belongs. For example, a claim of "greatly improved sample efficiency" sounds outstanding, but then it is not clear to a non-RL specialist how many order of magnitude we are talking about, and how augmentations themselves make the problem worse. While the paper deserves credit for exploring this subject in detail, such claims are vague at best and may not age well; sample efficiency may mean very different amounts of experience in 5 years time.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper presents a way of improving training stability of DrQ (an image-based RL algorithm that uses data augmentation) by applying image augmentation more carefully. The authors propose to input a mix of augmented and un-augmented observations into the critic, and stop augmenting the actor's inputs. On the considered benchmarks the proposed method demonstrates better generalization results.
reviews.main_review:
The paper identifies an issue with the increased variance of the target estimation in DrQ (image-based RL with data augmentation). The authors conjecture two root causes for the increased variance: non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization.
To remedy these issues they propose to apply data augmentation more carefully. Specifically, the authors suggest to mix augmented and un-augmented inputs to the critic and stop augmenting of the actor's inputs. This method is then empirically justified in a series of experiment on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite.

Pros:
* The author investigate an interesting question of an effect of data augmentation on generalization in image-based RL. 
* The method is thoroughly verified in different settings. 
* The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.
* The exploration of alternative architecture, such as ViT.

Cons:
* The paper proposes a small trick that lacks in novelty. The impact of data-augmentation on the estimation variance was also studied in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et. al), where a very similar technique was proposed -- the target estimation was done over 2 different augmentations. This work introduces a tiny modification of the DrQ's variance reduction approach, that just combines one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of combining two augmented observations. Furthermore, the authors fail to mention this prior result in their paper and compare to it. 
* From the paper it is not clear what hyper-parameters are used for the baselines, specifically for DrQ. If the authors use the original DrQ's parameters, then I see several major issues with the empirical results: 1) SVEA uses encoder arch that consists of 11 conv layers, while DrQ only uses 4. 2) The size of replay buffer of SVEA is 500K, while DrQ's 100K. 3) SVEA virtually increases the batch size by factor of 2, which usually leads to better performance on the DeepMind Control Suite tasks, it is not clear if the authors increase batch size for DrQ or not. These discrepancies alone could result in a drastic difference in performance and can render the empirical study to be invalid. 
* There is no experiments that compare individual components of SVEA to verify is that the improved performance comes from the proposed method or better/different hyper-parameters. 
* The authors claim in line 30 that they "theoretically ground" their findings, but this is not the case as there is no theoretical results presented. In general, the authors don't do a good job justifying that the two pitfalls are real. Their argument is hand-wavy and is based on a series of indirect comparisons to the baseline (DrQ). Unfortunately, there are too many moving parts in those comparisons that make it virtually impossible to validate the claim (see also the point above about the hyper-parameter discrepancies).

Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to mention the prior work that is almost identical, and the inconclusive empirical study I recommend to reject this paper from a high-profile venue of NeurIPS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

On the considered benchmarks the proposed method demonstrates better generalization results.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
The paper addresses a critical challenge in image-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) by investigating instability issues that arise when applying data augmentation (DA) to off-policy Deep Q-Learning algorithms. The authors identify two primary causes: high-variance Q-targets due to indiscriminate augmentation of successor states for bootstrapping, and over-regularization from Q-value estimation strictly based on augmented data. To mitigate these, they propose SVEA (Stabilized Q-Value Estimation under Augmentation), a framework that selectively applies augmentation only to the current state's Q-value estimation (not Q-targets) and introduces a modified Q-objective that jointly optimizes over both augmented and unaugmented observations, while the actor's inputs are not augmented. The method is empirically justified in a series of experiments on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite and robotic manipulation tasks, exploring both ConvNets and Vision Transformers (ViT).

**Main Review:**
The paper investigates an interesting and crucial problem in image-based RL: the challenges of stabilizing Deep Q-Learning with data augmentation for improved generalization. The authors identify two key issues contributing to instability: high-variance Q-targets from indiscriminate augmentation and over-regularization from Q-value estimation solely on augmented data. To address these, they propose SVEA, a method that suggests mixing augmented and un-augmented inputs to the critic and stopping augmentation of the actor's inputs. The exploration of alternative architectures, such as Vision Transformers (ViT), and demonstrating their application to image-based RL is a noteworthy aspect of the work. The paper is generally clearly written and easy to follow, with a logical narrative flow. The method is empirically justified in a series of experiments on tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite and robotic manipulation tasks, with a broad range of augmentations.

However, despite these identified issues and the proposed solution, a significant concern lies in the limited scope of novelty and technical contribution. The paper proposes what appears to be a small trick, which bears striking similarity to techniques previously explored in the original DrQ paper (Kostrikov et al.). Specifically, the prior work also studied the impact of data augmentation on estimation variance and proposed a very similar technique of estimating target values over multiple augmentations. This work introduces a minor modification by combining one augmented and one un-augmented observation instead of two augmented ones, yet it critically fails to mention this prior result or conduct a comparison, which is a major oversight.

Furthermore, the empirical study's validity is severely compromised by a lack of clarity regarding the hyper-parameters used for baselines, particularly DrQ. If original DrQ parameters were used, there appear to be significant discrepancies in architectural choices (e.g., SVEA's encoder arch consisting of 11 conv layers compared to DrQ's 4), replay buffer size (SVEA's 500K vs. DrQ's 100K), and effective batch size (SVEA virtually doubles it). These differences alone could drastically influence performance and render the empirical comparisons invalid. There is also a lack of experiments comparing individual components of SVEA to verify that the improved performance genuinely stems from the proposed method rather than better or different hyper-parameters.

The authors claim to "theoretically ground" their findings (line 30), but no theoretical results are presented. In general, the justification for the two identified pitfalls (non-deterministic Q-targets and over-regularization) is largely hand-wavy and based on indirect comparisons to baselines, which are themselves problematic due to the aforementioned hyper-parameter discrepancies. This makes it virtually impossible to validate the core claims.

Beyond these issues, the technical presentation suffers from several critical oversights and inaccuracies. Equation (2), defining the target parameter update, is incomplete as it misses the parameter symbol. Definition 1 (Optimality-Invariant State Transformation) makes a very strong and likely impractical assumption of exact equivalence for real-world augmentations; a more nuanced definition acknowledging approximation is needed. The description of Equation (3) as a 'nonnegative linear combination' is imprecise, and Equation (7) uses inconsistent notation (`k.k_2` for L2 norm) that does not align with the accompanying text. Moreover, the pseudocode in Algorithm 1, specifically lines 8-9, implies computationally inefficient element-wise sampling and augmentation within a batch loop, which misrepresents typical batched implementations. The claim of being "the first to successfully solve image-based RL tasks without CNNs" is extremely strong and requires more rigorous substantiation and a comprehensive survey of existing literature on Transformers in RL to avoid overstatement. While the paper is generally well-structured, these technical inconsistencies and minor grammatical errors (e.g., "in fact decrease sample efficiency" should be "decreases") accumulate to detract from the precision and clarity expected in a scientific publication.

Given the very limited scope of novelty and technical contribution, the failure to mention prior work that is almost identical, the inconclusive and potentially invalid empirical study due to hyper-parameter discrepancies, and the significant technical inaccuracies in the presentation, I recommend to reject this paper.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors include a "Broader Impact" section which briefly acknowledges concerns about the "growing ecological and economical footprint of deep learning – and RL in particular – with increasingly large models such as ViT." While it is commendable that they address this, the discussion is extremely brief and generic. It lacks a deeper exploration of specific negative societal impacts that might arise from their work, such as potential biases introduced or amplified by specific data augmentation choices, or the ethical considerations of deploying highly generalizable RL agents in sensitive real-world applications. A more thorough discussion, perhaps including how their method might mitigate or exacerbate these issues, would be beneficial. The paper could also expand slightly on the specific limitations of SVEA itself, such as the sensitivity of the method to its mixing coefficients (e.g., alpha, beta) or the types of augmentations it might still struggle with. Acknowledging that data augmentation, while helpful, does not fundamentally address shifts in dynamics or task objectives, and discussing its boundaries, would provide a more complete picture of the method's scope.

**Rating:**
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: On the Value of Infinite Gradients in Variational Autoencoder Models

reviews.summary: The paper investigates a specific work regime for VAE characterized by close to infinite gradients. The authors states that this phenomenon occurs for small values of a regularization power $\gamma$ and cannot be avoided, if one wants a perfect data reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:
- Clear analysis of presented phenomenon with a theorem, nice illustrations, profound investigation.

- Present explanation of a well-known effect during the VAE training.

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: not applicable

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents an investigation into a specific work regime for Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) characterized by the emergence of close to infinite gradients, particularly when the decoder variance parameter $\gamma$ approaches zero. The authors argue that, contrary to common perception of these as numerical instabilities, such unbounded gradients are provably required for achieving 'optimal sparse representations' of data lying on or near a low-dimensional manifold. An optimal sparse representation is defined as one that simultaneously achieves zero reconstruction error and maximally prunes uninformative latent dimensions. The paper provides a theoretical justification, including a key theorem, demonstrating that autoencoder-based energy functions necessitate unbounded gradients around optimal representations. It also highlights the practical benefits of learning $\gamma$ during training, suggesting it smooths the objective function and helps mitigate challenges associated with these gradients, leading to better-regularized latent spaces.

**Main Review:**

**Pros:**
The paper offers a highly original perspective on a known issue in VAE training—unbounded gradients—by reframing them as a necessary component for achieving optimal sparse representations. The analysis of this phenomenon is clear, supported by a central theorem (Theorem 4), nice illustrations, and a profound investigation into a well-known effect during VAE training. The theoretical framework, including Definition 1 and Theorem 4, provides a novel foundation for this perspective, shifting the focus from avoiding these gradients to understanding their fundamental role in achieving parsimonious latent representations. The submission is technically sound and well-argued in its theoretical development, with Lemma 2 and 3 effectively connecting the generic AE objective function to the VAE loss, establishing the theoretical framework for Theorem 4. The empirical results on CelebA, comparing learned vs. fixed $\gamma$, provide compelling evidence for the practical benefits of the proposed interpretation, especially concerning under-regularization.

**Cons:**
Despite its strengths, the paper suffers from several ambiguities, overly strong claims, and areas requiring further clarification or more rigorous support, which collectively diminish its overall impact and trustworthiness.

1.  **Generalizability and Scope:** The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice, the infinite gradient phenomenon can occur due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem in a broader context. While the core idea is novel, the concept of 'optimal sparse representations' itself, as defined, is quite specific, and its general applicability beyond the theoretical construct needs more nuanced discussion.
2.  **Theoretical Assumptions and Rigor:**
    *   Definition 1's requirement of 'zero reconstruction error' is an extremely strict theoretical ideal, particularly for continuous data and finite precision, which may not translate well to practical scenarios where 'negligibly small' is more realistic. This strong assumption might detach the theoretical results from practical realities.
    *   The assumption of an 'arbitrary encoder $\mu_z$ component' being unconstrained is also highly theoretical and potentially detached from the realities of neural network-based encoders, which have finite capacity and architecture-specific biases.
    *   Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general, potentially leading to bad solutions and invalidating further arguments. A discussion is needed on whether the infinite gradients phenomenon occurs even if the interpolation property is not strictly required.
    *   Theorem 4, a central contribution, states that 'there exists data X and decoder...' that *fails* to achieve optimal sparsity with bounded gradients. This is a weaker statement than the conclusion's 'unbounded gradients are in fact necessary for guaranteeing that global minima... will coincide with optimal sparse representations' for *all* cases, representing a logical gap that needs to be addressed.
    *   The intuition for Theorem 4 (Equation 9) relies on approximations that are not rigorously justified, especially when one variable is 'arbitrarily large' and others 'small', which undermines mathematical precision.
3.  **Specific Formulaic and Model Questions:**
    *   Formula (6) – why does the objective function have `h` in the first term, as usually we don’t use any `h()` for the training of AE? Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most `r` nonzero rows of Z (for example, if we impose only some regularization on `z` in the second term in (6))? Under which conditions on data do we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least `r<d` rows?
    *   The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if `sigma_z` is constant and `gamma` is small. Theorem 4 is proved for the optimization problem (6). While it is acceptable to transfer conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when `gamma` is small, the requirement of constant `sigma_z` is not obvious. What happens if `sigma_z` is modeled with some neural network and its parameters are tuned? Will the same effects be observed? Perhaps adaptive `sigma_z` could compensate for instability?
    *   The claim in Section 2.3 that optimal sparse representations are 'generally impossible' for $\beta$-VAE when $\beta > d/r$ needs a more robust, formal derivation rather than an inference from scaling arguments.
4.  **Context and Related Work:** The present work uses insights from B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018. I would expect some more explicit discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.
5.  **Practical Guidance:** I understand that in the case of sufficiently clean data lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune `gamma` to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning `gamma`, not just general advice? While learning $\gamma$ is presented as a smoothing mechanism, the paper could benefit from a more extensive discussion on practical, robust optimization strategies tailored for objectives with potentially infinite gradients, beyond general suggestions. A more thorough empirical investigation comparing its effectiveness with other established regularization or gradient clipping techniques would strengthen this aspect.
6.  **Empirical Validation and Clarity:** The experimental setups for Figures 3 and 4 are relegated entirely to the supplementary material, making it impossible to critically assess the validity and reproducibility of the empirical claims without external reference in the main text. The notation in Figure 4(b) showing 'dL(θ,φ)/dz' is ambiguous; it is unclear whether this refers to the gradient with respect to the latent variable `z` itself or a parameter influencing `z` (e.g., `φ`), and plotting a gradient with respect to a sampled latent variable is unconventional. For enhanced clarity, the main text could briefly summarize the network architecture and training details for the CelebA experiments in Section 4. A slightly more explicit explanation of how the functions `I∞(·)` and `log(·)` arise in Lemmas 2 and 3 from the VAE loss would also be beneficial.

In general, the paper is interesting and offers a valuable new perspective, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, necessitating more clarifications and rigorous support for its strong claims.

**Minor comments:**
*   Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?
*   Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?
*   The `(cid:0)` and `(cid:1)` artifacts from LaTeX conversion appear frequently, detracting from readability.
*   Figure 2's labels are exceptionally small, making the visual intuition difficult to grasp.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors commendably address several limitations within the paper. They discuss how $\beta$-VAE models with certain $\beta$ values ($\beta > d/r$) can prevent optimal sparse representations, and acknowledge that learning $\gamma$ is not a panacea, as it might exacerbate posterior collapse in some scenarios, potentially necessitating upper bounds on $\gamma$. They also note that their definition of optimality is not universal and primarily showcases regimes where infinite gradients are influential, and that it doesn't directly apply to adversarial outliers that mimic inlier manifold structure. However, the discussion of limitations feels somewhat understated given the strong theoretical assumptions made (e.g., zero reconstruction error). A more thorough discussion of when 'optimal sparse representations' (as defined) are truly achievable in real-world, noisy data scenarios with finite-capacity networks would strengthen the paper. For instance, if $\gamma$ does not converge to zero but to a small positive value (as hinted in the CelebA experiment), how does this affect the 'unbounded gradients are necessary' argument? This disconnect between the theoretical ideal and practical reality needs more explicit treatment.

Regarding societal impact, the paper does not explicitly include a dedicated section. However, the work is foundational and primarily theoretical, focusing on improving the understanding and training of generative models. Positive societal impacts could arise from more effective and robust VAEs, leading to advancements in areas like medical image analysis (e.g., better anomaly detection for diseases), scientific data generation (e.g., simulating complex systems), or creative applications. The risk of negative societal impact is indirect and generally associated with the broader use of generative models, such as the generation of misleading content or privacy concerns. The paper highlights the benefit of 'squeezing the latent space to the minimal number of informative dimensions needed for reconstructing inliers' to reduce the risk of outliers masquerading as inliers. However, if such a system were deployed, potential biases in the training data leading to certain groups being misclassified as 'outliers' due to subtle manifold differences or underrepresentation, could have negative consequences. A brief discussion on ethical considerations regarding the use of such models in high-stakes environments, even if only to acknowledge the need for careful data curation and bias mitigation, would be appropriate.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Cons:
- The paper provides only one possible reason for large gradients. However, in practice it can be the case, that infinite gradients phenomenon occurs due to different reasons, so there is no guarantee that the paper finally solves the problem.

- Optimization of gamma refers to Bayesian problem statement, thus a full Bayesian discussion of the problem at hand can provide more intuition on what is going on, and how can we avoid the infinite gradient effect with a proper prior on parameters.

- Formula (6) – why the objective function has h in the first term, as usually we don’t use any h() for the training of AE?
Maybe there exists another criterion for AE learning, that doesn’t require assumptions of Theorem 4 to get the perfect reconstruction with at most r nonzero rows of Z (for example, we impose only some regularisation on z in the second term in (6)?)? 
Under which conditions on data we get that $x^{(i)} = \mu_x(z^{(i),\theta})$ requires that $\|z_k\|_2>0$ for at least r<d rows?

- Does the infinite gradients phenomenon occur in other types of models? What makes this statement unique? What is with GMM or Kernel PCA or bayesian regression or PCA? E.g., in case of kernel PCA we can also require the data interpolation property. Will we observe the same problem?

- Assumption in formula (4) seems unrealistic given typically noisy settings for VAE and for statistics in general. It can lead to bad solutions making further arguments invalid. A discussion is needed.
Do we observe the infinite gradients phenomenon in case we do not require the interpolation property?

- The present work uses insights from 
B. Dai and D. Wipf. Diagnosing and enhancing VAE models. In ICLR, 2018.
I would expect some discussion on the relation of the new results and the results from that paper.

- I understand that in case of sufficiently clean data, lying on the manifold, the interpolation property is a reasonable requirement. According to the authors, we have to tune gamma to achieve it. Are there any specific algorithms for tuning gamma, not just general advices?

- The optimization problem in (6) follows from the VAE optimization problem (3) only if sigma_z is constant and gamma is small. Theorem 4 are proved for the optimization problem (6). I am OK to transfer the conclusions of Theorem 4 to the VAE case when gamma is small. But the requirement of constant sigma_z is not obvious. What does happen if we model sigma_z with some neural network and tune its parameters? Will we observe the same effects? Maybe adaptive sigma_z will compensate the instability?

In general, the paper is interesting, but the assumptions sometimes look too narrow or results are not formally connected, I need more clarifications.

Minor comments:
- Line 103 – should it be minimal instead of maximal?

- Line 325 - "spare representations". Maybe "sparse"?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
not applicable

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.
reviews.main_review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper studies the infinite gradient of training VAEs, namely when the decoder's variance is a learnable parameter, the variance will converge to zero, resulting in unbounded gradient. Although previously this is seen as an undesirable property, the authors prove that unbounded gradients are necessary for obtaining optimal spare representations. The authors also try to argue that learning the variance is better than fixing it to an arbitrarily small value.

Main Review:
The paper address an important issue in VAE training. It is widely observe that when the decoder variance is learnable, it will converge to a small number near zero. This paper make a nice connection between this phenomenon and optimal sparse representations, which is arguably the goal for learning an AE or VAE. By proving that it is inevitable to avoid the unbounded gradient while minimizing a generalization of AE loss, the adaptive decoder variance is justified.

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. When you study the learning of decoder variance, do you consider the effect of initialization? Typically when we learn the decoder variance, it is initialized as 1. However, what if we use other initializations, for example, initialize it at the convergence value, but still set it to be learnable? Maybe it is interesting to study different initializations using the dynamics in figure 4b.

2. I have a question regarding your statement that the using learnable gamma is important for improving sample quality. However, in my understanding, since learnable gamma will result in very small value of gamma, in the end the VAE model is still somewhat under regularized, and directly sample from it should not lead to good sample quality. Previously this is resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but I think the latent model is something else, and strictly speaking the sample obtained by two-stage models are not samples from the VAE. Maybe the authors can discuss a bit on this.

3. Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominanted by the reconstruction loss, and you show that learning gamma and fixing gamma at a small value leads to similar recon loss, do you observe similar performance of OOD detection when gamma is fixed at a small value? Intuitively they should behave similar here.


After rebuttal:
Thanks for addressing my concern, I will keep my score.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper investigates the often-observed phenomenon of unbounded gradients in continuous Variational Autoencoder (VAE) models, particularly when the decoder's variance (γ) is a learnable parameter and converges to zero. While previously seen as an undesirable property or numerical instability, the authors put forth a highly original perspective, arguing that these unbounded gradients are provably necessary for achieving optimal sparse representations of data lying on or near a low-dimensional manifold. They define optimal sparse representation as simultaneously achieving zero reconstruction error and maximally pruning uninformative latent dimensions. The paper provides theoretical justification, including a key theorem, demonstrating that autoencoder-based energy functions necessitate unbounded gradients around optimal representations to guarantee this form of parsimony. Furthermore, the authors contend that learning the variance is superior to fixing it to an arbitrarily small value, as it can smooth the objective function and help mitigate challenges associated with unbounded gradients, leading to better-regularized latent spaces and improved generative performance.

**Main Review:**
The paper addresses an important and well-known issue in VAE training, offering a highly original and counter-intuitive reinterpretation of unbounded gradients. Instead of proposing methods to avoid them, it reframes them as a fundamental, provably required component for achieving optimal sparse representations, a valuable property for manifold data. This connection between the vanishing decoder variance phenomenon and optimal sparse representations is a significant theoretical contribution. The central claim regarding the necessity of unbounded gradients for optimal sparse representations is rigorously proven in Theorem 4, which is a major strength and provides a novel foundation. The work clearly differentiates itself by shifting the focus from avoiding these gradients to understanding their fundamental role in achieving parsimonious latent representations, advancing the state of the art by fundamentally reinterpreting a long-standing "problem" in VAEs. The paper is generally well-written, professional, and logically structured, with effective figures illustrating complex concepts.

While the theoretical framework is compelling and technically sound, several questions and areas for further clarification remain regarding the practical implications and specific aspects of the theoretical claims:

1.  When studying the learning of decoder variance (γ), the paper could benefit from a more extensive discussion on the effect of initialization. Typically, γ is initialized at 1; however, exploring other initializations, such as initializing it closer to the convergence value while still allowing it to be learnable, might reveal interesting dynamics. This could be further studied using the dynamics presented in Figure 4b. Additionally, a more thorough empirical investigation comparing the effectiveness of learning γ with other established regularization or gradient clipping techniques would strengthen the practical recommendations.

2.  A key statement is that using learnable γ is important for improving sample quality. However, given that learnable γ often results in very small values, the VAE model might still be somewhat under-regularized, which intuitively should not lead to inherently good sample quality when directly sampled. The paper notes that previously this was resolved by training another generative model on the aggregated posterior, but clarifies that samples obtained by two-stage models are not strictly from the VAE. This points to a potential disconnect between the theoretical ideal of 'zero reconstruction error' (Definition 1), which is an extremely strict theoretical ideal for continuous data, and practical scenarios where 'negligibly small' is more realistic. A more explicit explanation of how this approach to learning γ prevents under-regularization and leads to better generative performance (e.g., non-blurry samples) would be beneficial. The paper's argument that learning γ smooths the objective and prevents convergence to under-regularized solutions is insightful, but could be elaborated upon in light of the practical implications for sample quality.

3.  Regarding the OOD detection experiments, since the log-likelihood (or equivalently BPD) of the VAE is largely dominated by the reconstruction loss, and the paper shows that learning γ and fixing γ at a small value leads to similar reconstruction loss, it would be important to observe and discuss whether similar performance in OOD detection is achieved when γ is fixed at a small value. Intuitively, they should behave similarly in this regard. Furthermore, the claim in Section 2.3 that optimal sparse representations are 'generally impossible' for β-VAE when β > d/r needs a more robust, formal derivation rather than an inference from scaling arguments. The central Theorem 4, which states that 'there exists data X and decoder...' that *fails* to achieve optimal sparsity with bounded gradients, is a weaker statement than the conclusion's implication that 'unbounded gradients are in fact necessary for guaranteeing that global minima... will coincide with optimal sparse representations' for *all* cases, representing a logical gap that needs to be addressed for enhanced rigor.

Beyond these specific questions, the clarity of presentation could be improved in several areas. The experimental setups for Figures 3 and 4 are relegated entirely to the supplementary material; briefly summarizing the network architecture and training details for the CelebA experiments in Section 4, even if full details are elsewhere, would enhance the paper's self-contained nature and allow for critical assessment of empirical claims without external reference. The notation in Figure 4(b) showing 'dL(θ,φ)/dz' is ambiguous; it is unclear whether this refers to the gradient with respect to the latent variable `z` itself or a parameter influencing `z` (e.g., `φ`), and plotting a gradient with respect to a sampled latent variable is unconventional. Additionally, the language often uses strong, definitive terms ('provably required', 'unequivocally no') but then qualifies them with 'per a certain technical sense' or 'generally be impossible', which can create a sense of imprecision.

After rebuttal:
The authors have addressed my concerns in the rebuttal, and I will maintain my original score.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors adequately address several limitations within the paper, acknowledging that learning γ is not a panacea and can have unintended consequences, such as exacerbating posterior collapse in some scenarios (potentially necessitating upper bounds on γ). They also note that their definition of optimality is not universal and primarily showcases regimes where infinite gradients are influential, and that it doesn't directly apply to adversarial outliers. However, a more thorough discussion of when 'optimal sparse representations' (as defined, particularly with the 'zero reconstruction error' requirement) are truly achievable in real-world, noisy data scenarios with finite-capacity networks would strengthen the paper. The disconnect between the theoretical ideal and practical reality needs more explicit treatment, especially if γ converges to a small positive value rather than strictly zero. While the paper suggests standard countermeasures like reduced step sizes for optimization challenges, a more extensive discussion on practical, robust optimization strategies tailored for objectives with potentially infinite gradients would be beneficial.

Regarding societal impact, the paper does not explicitly include a dedicated section. While the work is foundational and primarily theoretical, improving the understanding and training of generative models can have both positive and indirect negative societal impacts. Positive impacts could arise from more effective and robust VAEs, leading to advancements in areas like medical image analysis, scientific data generation, or creative applications. However, the broader use of generative models carries risks, such as the generation of misleading content or privacy concerns. Specifically, in applications like outlier detection, potential biases in training data could lead to certain groups being misclassified as 'outliers,' raising fairness concerns. A general statement acknowledging these broader ethical considerations, common to many machine learning applications, would enhance the review.

**Rating:**
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.
reviews.main_review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
