1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

Summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly.

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

This paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero, which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper presents a highly original and counter-intuitive perspective, arguing that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular, the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (implying a high-fidelity generative model), near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e., using unnecessary latent dimensions). The authors provide theoretical justification, including a key theorem, demonstrating that autoencoder-based energy functions necessitate unbounded gradients around optimal representations to guarantee a specific form of parsimony. Finally, the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA, suggesting that learning the decoder variance parameter (γ) during training can mitigate optimization challenges by smoothing the objective function and guiding the model towards better local minima.

**Contributions:**

1)  **Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance.** Significance: While the source of the problem—the Gaussian likelihood term being unbounded for variance approaching zero—has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature, the paper offers a novel reinterpretation. It reframes this known pathology as a necessary component for achieving optimal sparse representations, providing fresh insights into its connection to regularization.

2)  **A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression).** Significance: This definition is clear and relevant for specific applications, particularly training a high-fidelity generative model. However, it is an extremely strict theoretical ideal, especially for continuous and potentially noisy data. In practice, many cases exist where zero reconstruction error is undesirable for an optimal representation, as the data may contain noise or task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured. This needs more nuanced discussion, particularly concerning its general applicability beyond the theoretical construct.

3)  **Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture.** Significance: This claim, supported by Theorem 4, is presented as a significant novel contribution demonstrating that canonical autoencoder architectures *require* unbounded gradients to guarantee optimal sparsity. However, this claim is at best overstated, at worst potentially misleading, as the findings strictly apply to Gaussian likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distributions. Some reviewers identified ambiguities and logical gaps in the proof, suggesting that the conclusion that "unbounded gradients are necessary for guaranteeing that global minima... will coincide with optimal sparse representations" is a stronger statement than the theorem rigorously supports, which primarily states that *there exists data* for which optimal sparsity *fails* with bounded gradients.

### Main Review:

**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper offers a highly original and counter-intuitive reinterpretation of a previously pointed out pathology in VAE training. It argues that this pathology is unavoidable and, rather than working around it, should be incorporated into the optimization process, leading to a variance-smoothing scheme. The core idea of reframing unbounded gradients as beneficial rather than harmful is indeed novel. The technical part of the paper is generally well-written, and the central claim regarding the necessity of unbounded gradients for optimal sparse representations is rigorously proven in Theorem 4, a major strength.

However, the quality of the submission suffers from several ambiguities and overly strong claims that are not always rigorously supported or sufficiently qualified. Important conceptual assumptions are not discussed adequately; for instance, many works would disagree that optimal representations are always lossless. The definition of 'zero reconstruction error' is a strict theoretical ideal that may not translate well to practical scenarios with continuous, noisy data. Some claims are made concerning arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Gaussian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distributions. Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met, a point that needs to be clearly articulated throughout the paper.

While the prose is generally clear and accessible, the clarity of presentation can be improved in several areas. Mathematical notation is occasionally inconsistent or imprecise (e.g., `diag[σz]2` vs. `diag[σz^2]`). The presence of LaTeX conversion artifacts (`(cid:0)`, `(cid:1)`) detracts from readability. Figure labels, such as those in Figure 2, are exceptionally small, making the visual intuition difficult to grasp. The language often uses strong, definitive terms but then qualifies them, which can create a sense of imprecision.

The experiments shown are insightful, demonstrating the practical benefits of learning γ. However, since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, and comparisons to other methods are missing, it remains somewhat unclear how well the corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements. The lack of multiple repetitions means the statistical significance of the empirical findings remains unclear. Furthermore, the experimental setups for Figures 3 and 4 are relegated entirely to the supplementary material, hindering the paper's self-contained nature and reproducibility. The notation in Figure 4(b) showing `dL(θ,φ)/dz` is ambiguous, and plotting a gradient with respect to a sampled latent variable is unconventional.

### Verdict:

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct *under specific assumptions*, and the core idea of reinterpreting unbounded gradients is highly original. However, the findings are not entirely surprising given the Gaussian likelihood model, and some of the claims (e.g., no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. The technical quality, while having strong elements, also suffers from ambiguities in its central theoretical contribution (Theorem 4) and its supporting arguments, as well as an overly strict definition of 'optimal sparse representation' that may not be practically achievable.

My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well-behaved likelihood models—this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions with zero reconstruction error) is too broad—it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g., for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. Additionally, the limited experimental evidence and clarity issues in the empirical section further temper the impact of the work. I am therefore leaning towards acceptance, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However, since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper, it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
*   Highly original and counter-intuitive reinterpretation of unbounded gradients in VAEs, reframing them as a necessary component rather than a pathology.
*   Thorough theoretical analysis, including a rigorous proof (Theorem 4), of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions, the problem cannot be avoided.
*   Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process by learning the decoder variance (γ), which smooths the objective function.
*   Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations) that, while strict, is clearly articulated and relevant for specific applications.

**Cons:**
*   Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed priors), which are standard in parts of the VAE literature but limit generalizability.
*   Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods, which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
*   Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up; the single experiment lacks multiple repetitions, making the statistical significance unclear, and comparisons to other methods are missing.
*   The definition of 'zero reconstruction error' as a requirement for optimal sparse representations is an extremely strict theoretical ideal, potentially detached from practical scenarios with noisy data and finite-capacity networks.
*   Technical quality suffers from ambiguities and potentially overly strong claims in Theorem 4, which some reviewers found to have logical gaps or rely on unjustified approximations.
*   Lack of detailed descriptions for the experimental setups (e.g., specific architectures, datasets, hyperparameters) in the main text is a significant oversight, hindering the paper's self-contained nature and reproducibility.
*   Mathematical notation inconsistencies and LaTeX conversion artifacts detract from clarity.

**Improvements:**

1)  I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data—in fact, any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers on the topic of what constitutes good representations (e.g., The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000; Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018; Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018; Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017). This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar.
    1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.
    1b) Outlier detection with low γ (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e., perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2)  Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all, it addresses a widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. The community should also be encouraged to think more carefully about standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate.

3)  If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data, then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable. However, it should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modeling choice in such a situation. Alternatives should be discussed (e.g., flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives; readers will greatly benefit from that, and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4)  Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (information bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior.” Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used. I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal”—sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. Please discuss/clarify.

5)  Provide more detailed descriptions for the experimental setups, including specific architectures, datasets, and hyperparameters, directly within the main text or in a dedicated appendix section that is easily accessible. This will significantly improve reproducibility and allow for a critical assessment of empirical claims.

6)  Ensure consistent and precise mathematical notation throughout the paper, addressing issues like `diag[σz]2` vs. `diag[σz^2]` and clarifying the notation in figures (e.g., `dL(θ,φ)/dz`). Address LaTeX conversion artifacts.

**Comments:**

*   Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations—if they use Gaussian likelihoods, they will not prevent infinite gradients, of course (see also Improvement 4 above).
*   Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements), I think this statement needs to be weakened, or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed priors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g., lossy optimal representations).
*   The plotting of `dL(θ,φ)/dz` in Figure 4(b) is unconventional and its exact meaning should be clarified in the main text.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis). While the authors acknowledge that learning γ is not a panacea and can lead to posterior collapse, a more thorough discussion of when 'optimal sparse representations' (as defined) are truly achievable in real-world, noisy data scenarios with finite-capacity networks is needed. The paper could also benefit from a more extensive discussion on practical, robust optimization strategies tailored for objectives with potentially infinite gradients, beyond general suggestions. Regarding societal impact, while the work is foundational, improved VAEs could have implications in sensitive applications like outlier detection. Potential biases in training data leading to misclassification of certain groups as 'outliers' could have negative consequences. A brief discussion on ethical considerations regarding the use of such models in high-stakes environments, even if only to acknowledge the need for careful data curation and bias mitigation, would be appropriate.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
**Update after the authors' response**: I appreciate the detailed authors' response, and while it clarifies some points the paper remains a borderline case to me unless the applicability of the findings in the paper was clearly acknowledged and the breadth of the main claims was reduced accordingly. 

**Update after further engagement with authors**: We have managed to clear up some misunderstanding / misinterpretation from both sides, and I have raised my score accordingly from a 5 to a 6.

The paper investigates Autoencoders with a Gaussian likelihood model, where the variance is a trainable parameter. As has been observed before, this often leads to learned variances approaching zero (particularly for high-capacity models and noise-free data), which in turn leads to exploding gradients. The paper argues that these gradients and the collapse of the variance towards very low values should be embraced, rather than being seen as a pathology of the model. In particular the paper argues that under Gaussian likelihood and a fixed latent-space prior, if the goal is to learn a lossless representation of the dataset (which implies a high-fidelity generative model) near unbounded gradients are necessary to avoid underfitting (i.e. using unnecessary latent dimensions). Finally the paper proposes a scheme to mitigate optimization issues with unbounded gradients and illustrates the scheme on CelebA

**Contributions:**

1) Analyzing the origin of unbounded gradients of Gaussian likelihood (V)AEs with learnable (co-)variance. Significance: unsurprisingly, the source of the problem is the Gaussian likelihood term, which is unbounded for the variance approaching zero. The latter has been pointed out various times in the VAE literature and is a well known pathology when fitting Gaussian (mixture) models. 

2) A definition of optimal sparse representations as allowing for zero reconstruction error while only using a minimally required amount of dimensions (lossless compression). Significance: I think the definition can be useful for some particular applications (most notably training a high fidelity generative model), but in practice I would argue there are many clear cases where zero reconstruction error is clearly undesirable for an optimal representation (because the data contains noise or other task-irrelevant variations that should not be captured by the representation). This is well discussed in the literature on lossy compression and representation learning (rate distortion theory and information bottleneck literature and their modern application to neural networks and auto-encoder-like architectures).

3) Showing that, under said definition, “optimal sparse representations” are only attainable via near-unbounded gradients for any AE type architecture. Significance: I think this claim is at best overstated, at worst wrong: since Gaussian likelihoods are the root of the gradient explosion, the findings do not apply for other likelihood models that do not suffer from the pathology of unboundedenss (under a certain parametrization), such as e.g. Bernoulli likelihoods. The claim thus only applies to Gaussian likelihoods and other likelihood models with unbounded values, but not any AE architecture. Please correct me if my understanding (on the reliability of the findings on Gaussian likelihoods) is wrong.

Main Review:
**Originality, Quality, Clarity, Correctness:**

The paper follows up on a previously pointed out pathology, and shows that this pathology is unavoidable and rather than working around it (by limiting gradient magnitude), it should be incorporated into the optimization process (leading to a variance-smoothing scheme). The technical part of the paper is well written, but important conceptual assumptions are not discussed (many works would disagree that optimal representations are lossless), and some claims are made w.r.t. arbitrary AE models where, as far as I can tell, all the claims strictly only apply to Guassian-likelihood models (and other likelihoods that grow unbounded when fitting noise-free data) with fixed latent-space prior distribution (i.e. no aggregate posterior). Some of the claims (please correct me if I’m wrong) no longer apply when either of these assumptions is not met. The experiments shown are insightful, but since only a single experiment with the proposed variance-smoothing scheme is shown, it remains somewhat unclear how well the  corresponding results generalize and lead to claimed improvements - particularly since comparisons to other methods are missing and the sparsity of the solution obtained is not computed.

**Verdict:**

Overall I am on the fence with this paper. The analysis shown in the paper seems correct, but the findings are not very surprising, given that the likelihood model is Gaussian. Some of the claims (no optimal representation with any AE without near-unbounded gradients) sound very surprising, but only because they are stated without the explicit assumptions under which they hold. My two main concerns (see more details below) are: one, the analysis and results seem not to apply to well behaved likelihood models - this is never stated in the paper and should be clearly spelled out. Two, the definition of optimal representations (as lossless compressions) is too broad - it might be appropriate in some tasks, but it is clearly inappropriate in other tasks (where lossy compressions are desirable, e.g. for generalization, or under noisy data). This needs to be discussed thoroughly. I am therefore leaning towards rejection, noting that these issues can potentially be sufficiently addressed in the rebuttal. However since these main concerns regard multiple passages throughout the paper it is hard to judge whether a revised version of the manuscript would address the criticism sufficiently, without seeing the actual revised version. Either way, I am happy to revise my final verdict based on the other reviews and the authors’ response.

**Pros:**
 * Thorough analysis of the main problem and pointing out that under a number of assumptions the main problem cannot be avoided
 * Proposal on how to incorporate the problem into a more numerically stable optimization process.
 * Concrete definition of the goal (optimal sparse lossless representations)

**Cons:**
 * Claims stated in very general terms, despite depending on fairly specific assumptions (which, admittedly, are standard modelling assumptions in large parts of the VAE literature).
 * Main pathology results from unbounded likelihoods which has been identified as problematic before. Discussion of these previous works falls a bit short.
 * Experimental findings for the proposed variance-smoothing look OK but there is not a lot of experimental evidence to back them up, and the single experiment that was run does not have multiple repetitions such that the stat. significance of the empirical findings remains unclear (though I do expect most findings to be significant, the margins look fairly good).

**Improvements:**

1) I disagree with Definition 1 (in the broadest sense): optimal sparse representations for many tasks are produced via **lossy** compression, implying non-zero construction error. This includes tasks like representation learning, disentanglement, outlier detection with noisy data - in fact any task that assumes that the data is not noise-free (hence each datapoint contains some irrelevant information which should be removed via lossy compression). There are numerous papers the topic of what constitutes good representations, perhaps most notably
 * The Information bottleneck method, Tishby et al. 2000
But also:
 * Emergence of Invariance and Disentanglement in Deep Representations, Achille et al. 2018
 * Fixing a broken ELBO, Alemi et al. 2018.
 * Deep Variational Info Bottleneck, Alemi et al. 2017
This needs to be discussed in at least a paragraph, and the definition needs to be weakened to “optimal lossless representations for noise-free data” or similar (I’m not one of the authors, so certainly not asking for citations but would like to see a proper discussion).

1a) Clearly point out in which applications the lossless compression is desirable, and in which applications it is not desirable. Section 2.2 is currently biased towards one side.

1b) Outlier detection with low \gamma (and the illustration in Fig. 1) assumes (almost completely) noise-free data that lies on a smooth manifold perfectly captured by the model (i.e. perfect inter- and extrapolation). This rarely holds in practice and should be discussed in a more nuanced fashion.

2) Please (throughout the paper) make it clear that the main claims only apply to models with Gaussian likelihood (and trainable variance, but the latter is made sufficiently clear in the paper). Some of the claims sound like they would apply to any AE with arbitrary likelihood models. This is not meant to reduce the significance of the paper (after all I would say that the paper addresses the most widely used VAE model, hence it is of high significance) but to sharpen the focus and generalizability of the claims and results. I personally also think that the community should think more carefully about the standard modeling assumptions and search for alternative likelihood models whenever appropriate. The latter message is missing completely from the paper.

3) If the goal is to learn a high-fidelity image generator (or other generative model) or density modeling of noise-free data then a reconstruction error of 0 is indeed desirable, however I personally would question whether variational autoencoders are the right choice in these situations (admittedly they have been used in the literature in this context). It should be fairly obvious that a Gaussian likelihood with learnable variance is a wrong modelling choice in such a situation (I would even argue that having a fixed prior is often a wrong modelling choice, particularly when underfitting is an issue). Alternatives should be discussed (e.g. flow-based models, Bernoulli likelihoods, “aggregate posteriors” as priors, …). Please make an effort here to point out alternatives, I am convinced that readers will greatly benefit from that and it will help put the paper into wider context.

4) Sparsity as discussed in Definition 1 (ii) (line 105) is achieved by pushing the posterior towards the prior for non-informative dimensions. It has been discussed before (info bottleneck literature, fixing a broken ELBO) how this can be achieved by implementing a proper lossy compression scheme (following rate distortion theory) where, crucially, the prior is not fixed but given by the marginal or “aggregate posterior”. Famously, this leads to unneeded dimensions not being used (their distribution is the same as the prior which is the aggregate posterior). I therefore disagree with Line 327: “Hence there is no obvious alternative if this form of parsimony is our goal” - sparsity can also be achieved by using an aggregate posterior. This is also what Tomzcak and Welling 2018 do (but also e.g. Deep Variational Info Bottleneck which uses a variational approximation to the marginal). Please discuss/clarify.


**Comments:**

 * Line 345: I only partially agree. Adaptable/trainable priors should help with getting sparse representations - if they use Gaussian likelihoods they will not prevent infinite gradients of course (see also Improvement 4) above).
 * Line 219-220: Given my comments above (in the improvements) I think this statement needs to weakened or it needs to be clarified under what assumptions the statement holds (Gaussian likelihoods, fixed piors, and the precise definition of sparse optimal representation as Definition 1, but not for other, e.g. lossy optimal representations).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please add a paragraph with a focused discussion of the limitations of the proposed method (and perhaps reiterating the limited scope of the claims / theoretical analysis).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: Summary. This paper tries to analyze the regularization trade-offs for variational auto-encoding (VAE) models, in particular, explaining the infinite gradient phenomena in the training of VAE models. The author(s) defines some concepts to carry out the analyses.
reviews.main_review:
===== After Rebuttal =====

Please refer to my "After Rebuttal" post.

=====================

~~This is clearly not a NeurIPS level submission. I know it is quite frustrating to receive a rejection, so I have patiently read this manuscript until page 5, trying my best to prepare as much feedback as possible to help the author(s) improve this work, then I gave up. Its contents are simply not technically sound. I scrolled down to the Experiment section and found out it is not adequate as well, so here I am writing this review. The language issues in writing (sentences too long, grammar issues, bad expressions, etc.) I can tolerate them, but there are factual errors in this paper, so my evaluation is a firm no. The author(s) have some fundamental misunderstanding of variational inference models, as reflected in the definitions, analysis, and examples presented in this work. This work will be of limited value to the community. See more detailed comments in the limitation section below.~~
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
- Infinite gradients or overfitting? It turned out the infinite gradient issue the author(s) heavily focused on is caused by letting the precision parameter of the Gaussian noise model be trainable, which always explodes to infinity. Well, it is well known this is simply the overfitting in the finite sample scenario, and of course, it will drive the gradient to infinity. 
- The PCA example is inconsistent with the optimal sparse representation definition, as its latent representation has no uncertainty at all. 
- Reconstruction error is just an intuitive explanation of likelihood loss under the Gaussian observation model. For a proper theory of variational inference, the concepts should be instead defined by conditional likelihoods. 
- Please use vector formats instead of bitmaps in the Figures.
- The experiments need to be substantially enriched. 
- I do not feel the author(s) have shed real valuable insights to guide practice. What does the statement "this result suggests that heuristic modifications to or constraints on the VAE energy function may be ill-advised, and large gradients should be accommodated to the extent possible." even mean? Such confusing.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

- The experiments need to be substantially enriched. 
- I do not feel the author(s) have shed real valuable insights to guide practice. What does the statement "this result suggests that heuristic modifications to or constraints on the VAE energy function may be ill-advised, and large gradients should be accommodated to the extent possible." even mean? Such confusing.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper attempts to analyze the regularization trade-offs for variational auto-encoding (VAE) models, specifically focusing on the phenomenon of infinite gradients during VAE training. The author(s) introduce concepts to carry out this analysis, arguing that these unbounded gradients are not simply numerical instabilities but are provably necessary for achieving 'optimal sparse representations' in autoencoder-based architectures. This perspective aims to reframe a commonly perceived problem in VAEs.

Main Review:
This submission is unfortunately not at the level expected for a top-tier conference. While the paper proposes an original and counter-intuitive reinterpretation of unbounded gradients in VAEs, aiming to demonstrate their necessity for optimal sparse representations, the execution suffers from fundamental technical flaws and a lack of rigor. The core claims are not soundly supported, leading to conclusions that are of limited value to the community. The paper exhibits a fundamental misunderstanding of variational inference models, which is reflected in its definitions, analysis, and examples.

The central argument that unbounded gradients are "provably required" for optimal sparse representations is built upon a definition of "optimal sparse representation" (Definition 1) that demands "zero reconstruction error." This is an extremely strict theoretical ideal, particularly for continuous data and finite precision, and may not translate well to practical scenarios where a negligibly small error is more realistic. This theoretical ideal also seems detached from the realities of neural network-based encoders, which have finite capacity.

Furthermore, the central theoretical contribution, Theorem 4, which claims to demonstrate the necessity of unbounded gradients, appears to be a weaker statement than the strong conclusions drawn. The theorem states that *there exists* data and a decoder that *fails* to achieve optimal sparsity with bounded gradients, which is not equivalent to claiming unbounded gradients are necessary for *all* cases to guarantee global minima coincide with optimal sparse representations. The intuition provided for Theorem 4 also relies on approximations that are not rigorously justified, especially when variables are assumed to be "arbitrarily large" or "small," undermining mathematical precision. This methodological flaw significantly weakens the technical quality and the trustworthiness of the paper's core claims.

The language and presentation also pose challenges. While some sections are structured logically, the clarity of mathematical notation is inconsistent or imprecise (e.g., `diag[σz]2` vs. `diag[σz^2]`). Additionally, frequent LaTeX conversion artifacts (e.g., `(cid:0)`) detract from readability. These issues, while secondary to the technical flaws, further hinder the paper's ability to convey its arguments effectively.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper's treatment of the "infinite gradient" issue fundamentally misinterprets it. The phenomenon the author(s) heavily focus on, where the precision parameter of the Gaussian noise model explodes to infinity, is a well-known manifestation of overfitting in finite sample scenarios, which naturally drives gradients to infinity. The claim that these are "provably required" for optimal sparsity, particularly under the strict definition of zero reconstruction error, is not convincingly supported and stands in contrast to this established understanding. This disconnect between theoretical ideal and practical reality needs more explicit treatment.

Specific issues include:
*   **PCA Example:** The PCA example presented is inconsistent with the definition of optimal sparse representation, as its latent representation lacks any uncertainty.
*   **Variational Inference Fundamentals:** The paper defines concepts based on an intuitive explanation of likelihood loss under a Gaussian observation model, rather than the proper theoretical framework of variational inference, which should use conditional likelihoods. This suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of VAEs.
*   **Figures and Presentation:** Figures, such as Figure 2, have exceptionally small labels, making them difficult to interpret. The paper also uses bitmap formats for figures, which should ideally be vector formats for publication quality.
*   **Experiments:** The experimental section is notably inadequate and needs substantial enrichment. The setups for key empirical results (Figures 3 and 4) are entirely relegated to the supplementary material, making it impossible to critically assess their validity, reproducibility, or the specific architectures and hyperparameters used without external reference. The notation in Figure 4(b), showing `dL(θ,φ)/dz`, is ambiguous and unconventional for plotting gradients with respect to sampled latent variables. This lack of detail and rigor significantly diminishes the practical significance of the empirical claims.
*   **Practical Insights:** The paper fails to shed real valuable insights that could guide practice. Statements like "this result suggests that heuristic modifications to or constraints on the VAE energy function may be ill-advised, and large gradients should be accommodated to the extent possible" are confusing and lack actionable guidance, especially given the identified technical flaws that undermine the theoretical basis.

Regarding societal impact, the paper does not explicitly discuss potential negative implications. While VAEs are general-purpose generative models, their use in applications like outlier detection, as hinted in the paper, could have sensitive implications. If such systems, based on potentially flawed theoretical underpinnings, were deployed, biases in training data could lead to misclassification of certain groups as 'outliers,' raising fairness concerns. A more thorough discussion of ethical considerations and potential biases in high-stakes environments would be appropriate.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

reviews.summary: This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable.  They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditional gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g.  population covariance matrix, signal to noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is interesting to read. It belongs to the long list of papers that are concerned with the overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, they manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. They also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggests that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex model like neural networks. There is also an interesting example, where we can see that a frequently used interpolator, the minimum-norm interpolator, has arbitrarily bad generalization. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditional gradient descent highlight nicely the importance of initialization.

The paper could be of greater significance if
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator would not be restricted to the isotropic regime
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator.

In addition, I think that some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
In line:
-64  I don't think that Mourtada deals with interpolators in this paper.
-129 A comment regarding the subscript in the expectation may add some more clarity, as some times this means expected value given this quantity, or expected value given everything else other than this quantity
- 132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480  Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
This paper is concerned with inducing a certain type of best possible interpolator in linear regression. It is based on the notion of best possible interpolator, defined in Muthukumar et al., 2019., which is the best estimator (i.e., with minimum risk) among the class of interpolators. Based on that, the authors define a new theoretical estimator, the "optimal response-linear achievable interpolator" (wO), which is the best estimator among interpolators that are also linear in the response variable and do not depend on the true signal or noise. They manage to induce a closed form of this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization. In addition, using approximations for quantities that are usually unknown in practice (e.g., population covariance matrix, signal-to-noise ratio), they turn this theoretical estimator into a fully empirical one (wOe) and compare its performance to other frequently used estimators, showing that this estimator performs arbitrarily better in certain cases. Finally, they extend this notion of best possible theoretical estimator to the random features setting, where they again induce a closed form for this estimator and prove that it is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent under a specific initialization.

The paper is interesting to read and makes significant original contributions. It belongs to the long list of papers concerned with overparametrized estimation, but instead of trying to explain why this type of estimation generalizes so well, it aims to find the best linear interpolator in the linear regression setting. The definition and derivation of the "optimal response-linear achievable interpolator" (wO) is a novel contribution, offering a new benchmark for optimal generalization. By restricting the class of possible estimators in the definition by Muthukumar et al., 2019, to those linear in the response and independent of the true signal/noise, the authors manage to turn a theoretical device into a fully observable estimator. Its connection to preconditioned gradient descent with a specific initialization distinguishes it from prior work that often focuses on zero initialization or separate bias/variance optimization.

The paper demonstrates that wO can achieve arbitrarily better generalization performance compared to the minimum-norm interpolator and the optimal variance interpolator in certain regimes. This explicit demonstration of regimes where wO generalizes arbitrarily better than standard interpolators is a novel and impactful finding, challenging prevailing assumptions about the "goodness" of simple interpolators. This finding will likely spark further research into designing more sophisticated, distribution-aware interpolators for complex models. The authors also define a similar notion of optimal estimator for a more complex model (random features regression) and induce a closed form of this estimator, something that may suggest that we could try to induce this type of optimal estimators for even more complex models like neural networks. The extension to random features regression further highlights the generality of the proposed concept. Finally, these examples combined with the results related to preconditioned gradient descent nicely highlight the importance of initialization.

The paper is generally well-structured and written, with key definitions being precise and a logical flow from problem setup to main results and comparisons. The theoretical framework appears sound, with claims regarding generalization gaps rigorously supported by asymptotic risk calculations using random matrix theory (Section 5) and validated through numerical experiments.

The paper could be of greater significance if:
a) the construction of the fully-empirical estimator (wOe) would not be restricted to the isotropic regime.
b) (as they note) it included a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the estimation of the covariance matrix Σ. The authors commendably acknowledge that the lack of precise theoretical guarantees for the Graphical Lasso approximation (used in wOe) for general covariance matrices remains an open problem.
c) instead of performing an empirical analysis for special cases, they could manage to prove a theoretical result that bounds the generalization error of the fully-empirical estimator w.r.t. the generalization error of the theoretical optimal response-linear achievable interpolator. The current empirical validation of wOe, while promising, is restricted to specific synthetic scenarios, and a broader theoretical justification or extensive empirical validation across diverse settings would significantly strengthen its practical claims.

A significant methodological shortcoming is the absence of crucial error bars in the numerical experiments (Figures 1 and 2). This omission makes it challenging to assess the robustness and statistical significance of the observed performance differences, particularly for the critical claim that wOe performs "nearly identically" to wO, and fundamentally undermines the empirical claims' reproducibility and interpretation.

In addition, I think that some points could be written with more clarity or precision:
- The definition of 'response-linear achievable estimators' (Definition 3) could be more formally stated, explicitly clarifying the matrix-vector multiplication structure (e.g., w = A(X, Σ, Φ, δ)y) rather than relying on a subsequent descriptive sentence.
- Some points in the beginning of the proof of Proposition 1 could be written with more clarity.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Specific minor points for clarity and precision:
-64 I don't think that Mourtada deals with interpolators in this paper.
-129 A comment regarding the subscript in the expectation may add some more clarity, as sometimes this means expected value given this quantity, or expected value given everything else other than this quantity.
-132 "In linear regression (when d>n), minimizing the..."
-480 Maybe you could start with a general W that belongs in this class instead of Wo, as equation (17) seems a bit strange.

The authors have commendably acknowledged that the optimal interpolator wO requires knowledge of population parameters, and they proactively mitigate this by proposing the empirical approximation wOe. However, as noted, the lack of precise theoretical guarantees for the Graphical Lasso approximation for general covariance matrices remains an open problem. While the paper's primary focus is theoretical, and direct negative societal impacts are unlikely, improved understanding of generalization in overparametrized models can indirectly contribute to the development of more robust, reliable, and potentially fairer machine learning systems. A brief statement reflecting these potential positive broader implications would be a constructive addition.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolator in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.
reviews.main_review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolators in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.

**Main Review:**
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. The paper makes significant original contributions by formally defining and deriving the "optimal response-linear achievable interpolator" (wO), which is a novel class of interpolators characterized by being linear in the response variable and independent of the true signal/noise. This work introduces a novel, implementable notion of optimality within a practically relevant class of estimators. The submission is technically sound, providing solid theoretical foundations for wO and its algorithmic realization, with rigorous support from asymptotic risk calculations and numerical experiments. The paper is exceptionally well-written and organized, providing excellent context and motivation, with precise definitions and a logical flow that makes it easy to follow. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader.

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1.  The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. Its connection to preconditioned gradient descent with a specific initialization distinguishes it from prior work that often focuses on zero initialization or separate bias/variance optimization. The paper clearly articulates its distinction from prior work, particularly Amari et al. (2021). However, a more explicit discussion regarding how the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs, and whether techniques used there could be applied here to optimally balance bias and variance, would still be helpful to the reader.

2.  On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. A key contribution is showing that wO can achieve arbitrarily better generalization performance compared to the minimum-norm interpolator and the optimal variance interpolator in certain regimes. The explicit demonstration of regimes where wO generalizes arbitrarily better than standard interpolators (e.g., minimum-norm) is a novel and impactful finding. The discovery that the commonly used minimum-norm interpolator can generalize *arbitrarily worse* than wO is a critical insight that challenges existing assumptions about the "goodness" of simple interpolators. However, I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here, acknowledging existing work while emphasizing the novel aspects of their "arbitrarily worse" finding.

3.  Regarding the assumptions, both the noise xi and the true parameter w\* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w\* is not mean zero?

4.  In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w\_O is approximated by w\_{O\_e}, the paper proposes and empirically validates an approximation (wOe) that uses only training data, which is a strong point for practical applicability. The empirical approximation section (5.1) is particularly helpful for practical understanding. However, while the empirical approximation wOe is promising, its practical validation suffers from significant methodological shortcomings. The numerical experiments, though illustrative, lack crucial error bars, making it impossible to assess the robustness and statistical significance of the observed performance differences, especially for the critical claim that wOe performs "nearly identically" to wO. This omission fundamentally undermines the empirical claims. The empirical approximation wOe is only evaluated in specific synthetic scenarios, and a broader theoretical justification or extensive empirical validation across diverse settings is missing. Therefore, it would be beneficial to clarify what the structure of the population covariance matrix is in these experiments (isotropic, heavy-tailed, etc.), and to empirically verify whether the estimate Sigma\_e was close to Sigma, perhaps with a plot. Incorporating error bars in all experimental figures is strongly recommended for reproducibility and robust interpretation of the empirical findings.

=================== Post rebuttal comments ===================

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work. They adequately address the primary technical limitations, particularly acknowledging that the optimal interpolator wO requires knowledge of population parameters (Σ, δ, Φ), which are typically unknown in practice. They proactively mitigate this by proposing and empirically validating an approximation (wOe) that uses only training data, which is a strong point. They also correctly identify the lack of precise theoretical guarantees for the Graphical Lasso approximation (used in wOe) for general covariance matrices as a limitation and a potential area for future work. However, a more critical discussion of the empirical limitations is warranted, specifically the absence of error bars in all experimental figures, which prevents a robust assessment of the observed performance of wOe and the claimed "nearly identical" generalization. The empirical validation of wOe is currently restricted to a narrow set of synthetic examples, which limits the confidence in its general applicability. A deeper theoretical understanding of when such empirical approximations are guaranteed to perform well would strengthen the overall contribution.

Regarding societal impact, while the paper's checklist marks it as "N/A" and direct negative societal impacts are unlikely for this theoretical work, the broader context of understanding generalization in complex, overparametrized models like neural networks does carry societal implications. Developing more robust and better-generalizing interpolators could indirectly contribute to the development of more reliable, fairer, and robust machine learning models in applications where interpolation is critical. This could potentially reduce reliance on specific data characteristics or improve performance on out-of-distribution data, thereby mitigating certain biases. Conversely, if optimal interpolators are computationally intensive or require specific data characteristics, this could exacerbate existing inequalities in access to advanced ML technologies. A brief discussion on these potential broader implications, even if largely positive or requiring further investigation in the context of more complex models, would be a constructive addition.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization error of a class of interpolator in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality. They consider a setting where there is a true linear model generating the data, whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero-mean and covariance Phi. In this setting they consider a natural class of estimators that are a function of the data, the population covariance matrix, the prior covariance matrix Phi and the signal to noise ratio. Among this class of estimators they calculate a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator, with respect to the expected excess risk and show that there are certain settings where the expected excess risk of this estimator can be much lower than the risk of the OLS solution. They also provide an extension of their results to the random features model.

Main Review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
reviews.main_review:
**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Summary:
The paper under reviews studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation. More precisely the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs and as a function of desired quantities. From this, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator from a optimization algorithm. Finally they expose cases where the designed estimator show some good generalization properties while the miminum norm interpolator does not.  The link with kernel methods is also made.
