reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.
reviews.main_review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formally formulate the active voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, where each PV inverter acts as an independent agent, and introduce a comprehensive open-source environment with three realistic scenarios using real-world load and PV data. They implement a number of different MARL baselines and investigate the impact of various voltage loss functions, including a novel 'Bowl-shape' function, on their performance. The work also compares MARL-based control with traditional methods, aiming to bridge the gap between the power systems and MARL communities.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a highly important and timely problem, and that the development of a comprehensive open-source environment for multi-agent active voltage control is a significant contribution that would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities. The problem formulation as a Dec-POMDP for individual inverters is a notable effort, and the extensive experimental comparison of seven MARL algorithms across different scales and reward functions provides valuable insights into their applicability and limitations in this real-world domain.

On the other hand, I find that one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, there should be more explicit details about the actual usability of the environment, and some of the technical notations, explanations, and mathematical definitions need to be significantly cleaned up and clarified. While the paper makes a valuable contribution by providing a benchmark, the overall significance is tempered by identified technical and clarity issues, and the somewhat underwhelming performance of MARL compared to traditional methods in certain cases, coupled with interpretability challenges.

**Originality:** The core contribution of formalizing active voltage control as a Dec-POMDP with each inverter as an independent agent, and developing a comprehensive open-source environment for this specific problem, is valuable for bridging the power and MARL communities. The introduction of the 'Bowl-shape' voltage barrier function is presented as novel; however, its mathematical definition, along with those for the 'L1-shape' and 'L2-shape' baselines, is conspicuously absent from the paper. This omission makes it impossible to fully understand or reproduce these crucial components, significantly hindering its contribution. Furthermore, while the rigorous application and comparative evaluation of MARL in this critical domain represent a valuable effort, the fundamental concept of using learning-based control for power systems is not entirely new.

**Quality:** The submission is technically sound in its overall approach, and claims are largely supported by thorough experimental results on three distinct network topologies using realistic load and PV generation data. The chosen MARL algorithms and traditional benchmarks are relevant, and the authors are commendable for being honest about the limitations of MARL, such as interpretability and robustness, noting instances where MARL does not significantly outperform or even underperforms traditional methods. This honesty strengthens the paper's scientific integrity.

However, the technical presentation suffers from several significant oversights that detract from its quality and reproducibility. As mentioned, the mathematical definitions for the proposed 'Bowl-shape' barrier function, as well as the 'L1-shape' and 'L2-shape' baselines, are entirely missing. The claim of transforming a constrained reward to an unconstrained one via KKT conditions is made without any derivation or reference. The action set definition in footnote 5 is confusing and contradictory regarding whether `ak` represents reactive power or its change. The `qPV` vector in the `lq(qPV)` term of the reward function is not explicitly defined. Additionally, the number of random seeds (5) used for experiments is quite low for MARL, potentially leading to less robust conclusions. While the modification of COMA for continuous actions is mentioned, its details are relegated to an appendix, which is acceptable but still requires careful scrutiny given the other missing details.

**Key Assumption:** One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks; that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

**Usage Instructions:** Since a key contribution is an open-source environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no explicit details relating to the practical usability of the environment. While the provision of an open-source environment is excellent for reproducibility and a significant contribution, details such as whether it integrates within existing MARL frameworks, if it has a user interface, or if a quickstart guide is planned are crucial. These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this environment would actually be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

**Terminology and Explanations:** As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
*   Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
*   Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
*   Line 140: Define "per unit"
*   The observation set `Oi = (h ◦ f ◦ g)(i )` is presented with excessive abstraction, lacking concrete examples of what an agent actually observes.

**Clarity (General):** The paper contains numerous minor grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., "drive force" instead of "driving force," "comes under spot light" instead of "comes into the spotlight," "Muti-stage transformers" instead of "Multi-stage transformers"). The figure captions for Figures 5 and 6 use inconsistent and somewhat unclear terminology (e.g., "BL is the contraction of Bowl"). The placement of footnote references (e.g., "environment.2") is sometimes incorrect. These issues, alongside the missing mathematical details, significantly detract from the paper's clarity and reproducibility for an expert reader.

**Other Questions and Comments:**
*   Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
*   Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
*   Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
*   Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
*   Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
*   Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods, especially given that the paper's own findings indicate MARL often fails to significantly outperform, and sometimes performs worse than, conventional methods.

**Minor:**
*   Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
*   Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
*   Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have adequately addressed these points in their 'Discussion' and 'Conclusion' sections, highlighting critical challenges such as the significant impact of voltage barrier function design, the scalability of MARL, and the paramount issues of interpretability and robustness. They explicitly acknowledge that MARL can generate "inexplicable behaviours" and that an "interpretable and trustable algorithm is highly desired for industrial applications," which is commendable honesty.

However, the paper could benefit from a more explicit and deeper discussion on potential negative societal impacts. While the authors responsibly frame active voltage control as "less sensitive to reliability" than other power network problems, the specific consequences of voltage deviations (e.g., equipment damage, reduced service life, power quality issues for consumers) should be elaborated. The potential negative impacts of deploying non-interpretable or less robust MARL solutions in critical infrastructure, even for "less sensitive" problems, warrant further exploration. Additionally, the computational resources (energy consumption) required for training and deploying these MARL algorithms, especially at scale, is not discussed, which is a relevant societal and environmental consideration. Future work should explore formal verification or safety-aware MARL techniques to provide stronger guarantees, addressing the societal need for reliable and safe power systems.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.
reviews.main_review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).

## Hybrid Peer Review

Summary:
This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP, where each PV inverter is modeled as an independent agent. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements from sensors in their operating region to maintain voltage within a safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks, utilizing real-world load and PV data. The paper also investigates different reward functions, including a novel 'Bowl-shape' function, and compares MARL performance against traditional control methods like Optimal Power Flow (OPF) and droop control. The work aims to bridge the gap between the power systems and MARL communities.

Main Review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms, consisting of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies (ranging from 6 to 38 agents across 33-bus, 141-bus, and 322-bus networks) and real-world data, providing a comprehensive and realistic scenario where RL methods can be tested. This is a significant effort in formally defining the active voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP and developing an open-source testbed that will be valuable for fostering interdisciplinary research and accelerating progress in applying MARL to power network control. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. The extensive experimental comparison of seven state-of-the-art MARL algorithms, alongside the evaluation of different voltage barrier functions and comparison with traditional methods, provides useful insights into MARL's capabilities and limitations in this domain. The authors are commendably honest in their assessment, acknowledging interpretability and robustness challenges and noting instances where MARL does not consistently outperform or even underperforms traditional methods.

However, a major concern that significantly impacts the utility of this work for the community is the absence of the code in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment, especially for a paper whose primary contribution is a new benchmark domain. This seriously hinders reproducibility and widespread adoption.

Furthermore, while the paper introduces a novel 'Bowl-shape' voltage barrier function and evaluates L1 and L2 baselines, the mathematical definitions for these crucial components are conspicuously absent from the paper. This makes it impossible for readers to understand or reproduce these reward functions, which are central to the experimental setup. Relatedly, the claim of transforming a constrained reward to an unconstrained one via KKT conditions is made without any derivation or reference, and the action set definition in footnote 5 is confusing and contradictory regarding whether `ak` represents reactive power or its change. The `qPV` vector in the `lq(qPV)` term of the reward function is also not explicitly defined. These technical oversights detract from the paper's clarity and scientific rigor.

I also miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run, i.e., what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions, i.e., how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others, and why MARL often performs worse than traditional methods for certain cases, despite its complexity.

It is not entirely clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters, though one alternative review clarifies that each PV inverter is modeled as an independent agent. However, the question remains: why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy?

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day). The number of random seeds (5) used for experiments is also quite low for MARL, potentially leading to less robust conclusions. While the modification of COMA for continuous actions is mentioned, its details are relegated to an appendix, hindering full reproducibility.

Overall, while the problem formulation and the creation of an open-source environment for a highly significant problem are valuable contributions, the immediate practical utility of MARL is tempered by its acknowledged performance limitations compared to traditional methods, coupled with critical issues regarding interpretability and robustness. The identified technical omissions and clarity issues, particularly the missing code and mathematical definitions for reward functions, significantly reduce the paper's impact and reproducibility for an expert reader.

Minor details:

*   `h` is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228). This needs to be clarified.
*   The paper contains numerous minor grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., "drive force" instead of "driving force," "comes under spot light" instead of "comes into the spotlight," "Muti-stage transformers" instead of "Multi-stage transformers").
*   Figure captions for Figures 5 and 6 use inconsistent and somewhat unclear terminology (e.g., "BL is the contraction of Bowl").

Rating: 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
The article presents an open-source environment for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) based active voltage control, aiming to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community. While the development of an open-source environment with multiple realistic scenarios (33-bus, 141-bus, 322-bus) using real-world data is a significant contribution that could serve as a valuable benchmark, the authors appear to have overlooked existing efforts like L2RPN, and their approach to standardization is not sufficiently comprehensive.

**Main Review:**

The paper makes an effort to establish a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL-based active voltage control. However, the effort for standardization, while commendable in its intent to provide a benchmark for future research, is not adequately executed. A truly standard framework for RL-based learning in smart grids requires more rigorous specifications than currently provided. This includes:
*   Clear definition of actions and their domains, which currently appear restricted primarily to reactive power, a limitation that the authors do not fully acknowledge as potentially affecting results compared to approaches using a wider range of actions. The action set definition in the paper's footnote 5 is confusing and contradictory regarding whether `ak` represents reactive power or its change, further obscuring this crucial aspect.
*   Precise definition of states and the effect of grid-users on these states. The observation set `Oi` is presented with excessive abstraction, lacking concrete examples of what an agent actually observes.
*   A standard definition of the environment and its consistent response to actions.
*   Defined standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects.

Furthermore, for a versatile benchmark environment, the algorithms and networks need to be fully customizable to allow for diverse research avenues.

The problem formulation itself is sketchy in several key areas. As noted, the actions are primarily dependent on reactive power. While the paper does explore the impact of different voltage barrier functions, including a novel 'Bowl-shape' function alongside L1 and L2, the mathematical definitions for these crucial functions are entirely missing from the paper. This absence makes it impossible to understand or reproduce these components, severely hindering the clarity and quality of the work. Similarly, the claim of transforming a constrained reward to an unconstrained one via KKT conditions is made without any derivation or reference, and the `qPV` vector in the `lq(qPV)` term of the reward function is not explicitly defined. These omissions undermine the technical soundness and reproducibility of the proposed approach.

The authors also appear to have overlooked the significant impact of network layers on algorithm performance, a factor that is well-established in the literature (e.g., https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz1). Additionally, the effect of replay selection on results seems to be underestimated, which may contribute to the authors' own doubts about their statements (e.g., lines 302-303, and section 5.3). This is particularly relevant given their choice of reward function, which they name the 'Bowl function' but could also be interpreted as a parabolic function. The extensive experimental comparison of seven state-of-the-art MARL algorithms across different scales and reward functions is a strong point, yet the lack of transparency in the definition of these reward functions diminishes its impact.

The paper's technical quality is further impacted by the use of only 5 random seeds for experiments, which is quite low for MARL and potentially leads to less robust conclusions. While the modification of COMA for continuous actions is mentioned, the details are relegated to an appendix and still lack sufficient elaboration for full reproducibility.

Overall, the manuscript contains numerous minor grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., "drive force" instead of "driving force," "comes under spot light" instead of "comes into the spotlight," "Muti-stage transformers" instead of "Multi-stage transformers"). The figure captions for Figures 5 and 6 use inconsistent and somewhat unclear terminology (e.g., "BL is the contraction of Bowl"), and the placement of footnote references is sometimes incorrect. These issues collectively detract from the clarity and professionalism of the presentation. The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much substantial value to the article in their current form.

The paper's own findings, which are honestly presented, reveal that MARL approaches often fail to significantly outperform, and sometimes perform worse than, conventional methods like Optimal Power Flow (OPF) and droop control, particularly regarding robustness and interpretability. This observation, alongside the identified technical and clarity issues, limits the immediate practical significance for power system operators who prioritize safety and reliability. The work serves more as a foundational effort for future MARL research in this area, rather than an endorsement of current MARL capabilities for real-world deployment.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors are commended for their honesty in discussing limitations within their 'Discussion' and 'Conclusion' sections, highlighting the significant impact of voltage barrier function design on algorithm performance, the diverse and sometimes inexplicable behaviors of MARL compared to traditional methods, and the ongoing issues of interpretability and robustness. They explicitly acknowledge that MARL can generate "inexplicable behaviours" and that an "interpretable and trustable algorithm is highly desired for industrial applications."

However, the discussion on limitations needs to be more elaborate regarding the proposed technique/framework and the specific limitations of their solution within that framework. While the paper implicitly focuses on the positive aspects of MARL for improving power grid stability, a more explicit discussion of potential negative societal impacts is warranted. Even if active voltage control is considered "less sensitive to reliability" than other power network problems, the potential consequences of voltage deviations (e.g., equipment damage, reduced service life, power quality issues for consumers) and the risks associated with "inexplicable behaviours" in critical infrastructure should be further explored. Furthermore, the computational resources (energy consumption) required for training and deploying these MARL algorithms, especially at scale, is a relevant societal and environmental consideration that is not discussed. Future work must prioritize demonstrating extreme robustness, formal safety guarantees, and clear interpretability before MARL could be considered for deployment in such critical systems.

**Rating:**
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents a novel method for releasing differentially private samples drawn from a Dirichlet posterior distribution. The authors provide a closed-form privacy guarantee for their proposed mechanism using truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP), which is subsequently converted into an approximate differential privacy guarantee. The work includes a comprehensive privacy analysis with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ and derives analytical utility guarantees in terms of sample size for two practical applications: Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Through empirical evaluation, the authors demonstrate that their proposed Dirichlet mechanism outperforms the Gaussian mechanism under specific conditions, particularly when the number of samples is small or for sparse histograms.

**Main Review:**
The paper proposes a method that draws a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior distribution, specifically from Dir($x + \alpha$), which represents the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir($\alpha$) for the multinomial probabilities. The core contribution lies in the explicit and closed-form tCDP guarantees derived for this Dirichlet posterior sampling. This detailed characterization for a widely used distribution is a valuable and novel contribution, building upon existing, more general frameworks for exponential families. The authors clearly differentiate their work from prior studies, highlighting their distinct approach of incorporating prior parameters to achieve privacy guarantees, in contrast to input space restrictions used in other mechanisms.

The analysis presented in the paper appears novel and constitutes a solid contribution to privacy research. I appreciate the authors' efforts in deriving analytical expressions connecting privacy and utility. The technical derivations for tCDP and approximate DP are generally well-presented, with a clear proof sketch for Theorem 1, and the mathematical tools, such as polygamma functions, are appropriately applied. The utility analysis is comprehensive, offering theoretical bounds for KL-divergence and L-infinity error, and a thorough discussion of sample size requirements across different privacy regimes. The comparative analysis with the Gaussian mechanism is well-executed and provides practical insights, demonstrating the Dirichlet mechanism's advantage for small sample sizes or sparse data. The authors are commendably transparent about the limitations of their bounds and the trade-offs involved, suggesting a complete and honest evaluation.

While the method's use cases might be rather limited (e.g., the histogram release demonstrated in Section 4.2), the rigorous analysis makes this a strong privacy paper. The identification of conditions where the Dirichlet mechanism outperforms the Gaussian mechanism provides actionable guidance for practitioners.

**Pros:**
*   (+++) Very clearly written and well-structured paper.
*   (++) Novel and explicit closed-form privacy analysis in terms of the Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$ using tCDP.
*   (++) Comprehensive utility analysis in terms of sample size, including theoretical accuracy guarantees.
*   (+) Empirical results demonstrating improvement over the Gaussian mechanism, particularly for small N or sparse histograms.
*   (+) Technically sound derivations and well-supported claims.

**Cons:**
*   (--) The method's primary use cases might be somewhat limited, though valuable for specific applications.
*   (--) The actual difference between the two use cases presented in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 could be more distinctly articulated.
*   (--) Minor technical and clarity points, as detailed below, require attention for refinement.

**Minor comments/questions for refinement:**
*   **Introduction, lines 30-32:** Clarify if the discussion pertains to drawing from the posterior predictive distribution; if so, the role of Y should be explicitly stated.
*   **Theorem 1, proof:** In the proof sketch (line 169), `u' := x' + α'` appears to be a typo and should likely be `u' := x' + α`. Additionally, a point regarding the monotonicity of `ψ'(x)` in the proof sketch (L177) has been noted as requiring re-examination for explicit clarity, though the overall technical soundness of the theorem is broadly supported. Clarification on the adjacency definition for `x` and `x'` in Theorem 1 would also be beneficial, explicitly connecting it to underlying adjacent datasets.
*   **Approximations in Sections 4.1 and 4.2:** The approximations for `α` in these sections could benefit from a careful re-check of constant factors to ensure maximal precision.
*   **Notation consistency:** The notation `∆2` vs `∆2^2` is inconsistently applied throughout the paper (e.g., Theorem 1, Section 4.1, 4.2) and should be standardized. It would also be beneficial to explicitly state the interpretation of ∆2 and ∆∞ sensitivities in the context of histogram inputs, e.g., that a single record change in the underlying dataset implies ∆2=1 and ∆∞=1 for the histogram counts `x`.
*   **Figure 2:** The y-axis label "$\ell_2$-accuracy" is potentially confusing; "$\ell_2$-error" would be more appropriate. Similarly, the phrase "smaller (l_inf)-accuracy" in Section 4.2 (L280-281) could be rephrased for clarity to avoid misinterpretation, aligning with standard error metrics.
*   **General Clarity:** Attention to typesetting and proofreading is needed to remove minor LaTeX artifacts (e.g., `(cid:81)`, `(cid:15)`) scattered throughout the text. The overview section uses inconsistent formatting (numbered list vs. bullet points). Figure 1's labels could be made clearer to enhance interpretability. The description of `ψ'(x)` approximations in Section 2.4 could be more mathematically precise.
*   **Checklist response:** The paper's checklist response regarding complete proofs for Theorem 1 could be more consistent with the stated "proof sketch that skips some calculations" rather than claiming proofs are in the main paper.
*   **Proof of Theorem 1:** While a sketch is provided, considering the centrality of Theorem 1, including the full proof directly in the main text rather than solely in the appendix might enhance readability for some readers.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The authors have commendably addressed both the technical limitations and potential negative societal impacts of their work. They clearly state that the derived privacy bounds, while worst-case, can become loose when input `xi` values are large. They also provide a nuanced comparison to the Gaussian mechanism, identifying scenarios where the Gaussian mechanism might offer superior accuracy, thus guiding practitioners in mechanism selection. The discussion on societal impact is particularly strong, emphasizing the ethical responsibility of organizations to be transparent about privacy parameters and to educate users. Crucially, the authors highlight the risk of disparate impact on minority groups if uniform priors are carelessly applied, advocating for thoughtful prior parameter selection to mitigate bias and ensure fairness. The suggestion to "privately impose larger prior parameters on larger populations" is a constructive idea for mitigating this issue, although the practical implications of "privately imposing" such parameters would require careful consideration to avoid introducing new privacy risks or biases. One additional point to consider for future work is providing guidance on robust practical selection methods for optimal prior parameters ($\alpha$) and the $\gamma$ parameter in tCDP for real-world datasets, especially given the acknowledged divergence of the upper bound for large values of sample statistics.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.

Main Review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: Summary: This paper gives a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has a single main result, namely a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of the algorithm which takes a sensitive data set as input and a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution on the parameters, and samples a single value from that distribution. 

The main result (Theorem 1) is stated in terms of truncated concentrated DP, though I think it might be easier to state it in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. (I also think it would be worthwhile, in the corollaries, setting $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to get clear statements.) 

I have very few comments on the writing: the paper is clear and easy to understand and, I think, accurately represents and discusses the contributions. The only question here is significance: the paper is providing updated calculations for a well-know idea. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue. I’m less sure that it meets the bar for NeurIPS. If the committee ultimately decides not to accept the paper, I would encourage the authors to submit to an appropriate journal.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

Summary:
This paper provides a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set. The core contribution is the derivation of a closed-form privacy guarantee using truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP) for Dirichlet posterior sampling, which is then converted into an approximate differential privacy guarantee. The authors further analyze the utility of this privacy mechanism in two practical applications: Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing, offering theoretical accuracy guarantees and a comparative analysis with the Gaussian mechanism.

Main Review:
The paper's central contribution is a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of an algorithm that takes a sensitive data set as input, applies a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution, and samples a single value from it. This work makes a valuable contribution by providing explicit and closed-form tCDP guarantees specifically for Dirichlet posterior sampling, building upon existing, more general frameworks for exponential families and addressing a critical gap in the literature. The authors' approach of adding prior parameters to the Dirichlet mechanism, contrasting with input space restrictions in other works, also demonstrates a distinct and original methodology.

The theoretical derivations for tCDP and approximate DP are generally well-presented, with a clear proof sketch for Theorem 1 and a reference to the appendix for a full proof. The mathematical tools, such as polygamma functions, are appropriately applied. The utility analysis is comprehensive, offering theoretical bounds for KL-divergence and L-infinity error, and a thorough discussion of sample size requirements across different privacy regimes. The comparative analysis with the Gaussian mechanism is well-executed and provides practical insights, particularly highlighting scenarios where the Dirichlet mechanism is advantageous (e.g., sparse histograms).

However, the technical quality of the manuscript is unfortunately impacted by several significant issues that require immediate attention. Foremost, the proof sketch for Theorem 1 incorrectly states that `ψ'(x)` is an increasing function, when it is in fact decreasing for `x > 0`. This fundamental error undermines the derivation of the lower bound `αm − γ` and casts doubt on the validity of the entire theorem. Furthermore, the derivation of `α` approximations in both the high- and low-privacy regimes in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 appears to consistently miss factors (e.g., of `2` or `sqrt(2)`). The notation `∆2` vs `∆2^2` is inconsistently applied throughout the paper (e.g., Theorem 1, Section 4.1, 4.2), creating confusion as to whether `∆2` denotes sensitivity or squared sensitivity. The adjacency definition for `x` and `x'` in Theorem 1 is also somewhat ambiguous, lacking explicit connection to underlying adjacent datasets. These are not minor stylistic points but represent significant technical deficiencies that must be addressed for the results to be reliable.

Regarding the main result, it might be clearer to state Theorem 1 in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. It would also be worthwhile, in the corollaries, to set $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to obtain clearer statements.

The manuscript is generally clear and well-structured, with an effective introduction that motivates the problem and outlines the paper's contributions, and logically presented main results. However, clarity is hampered by numerous presentation issues. A pervasive problem is the presence of numerous LaTeX artifacts (e.g., `(cid:81)`, `(cid:15)`) scattered throughout the text, including in equations, suggesting a lack of careful typesetting and proofreading. The overview section uses inconsistent formatting. Figure 1 contains confusing labels, making interpretation difficult. The description of `ψ'(x)` approximations in Section 2.4 is mathematically imprecise. The phrase "smaller (l_inf)-accuracy" in Section 4.2 (L280-281) is confusing and appears to be used incorrectly. Furthermore, the checklist response regarding complete proofs (2b) is self-contradictory, as Theorem 1's proof is explicitly stated as a "short proof that skips some calculations" yet the checklist claims proofs are in the main paper. Minor clarity improvements could include clarifying the distinction and usage of `ρ` and `ρ'`, and explicitly stating the interpretation of `∆2` and `∆∞` sensitivities in the context of histogram inputs (e.g., that a single record change implies `∆2=1` and `∆∞=1` for histogram counts `x`). While a full proof of Theorem 1 is referenced in the appendix, given its centrality, considering its inclusion directly in the main text could enhance understanding.

The problem addressed by the paper—quantifying differential privacy for Dirichlet posterior sampling—is of considerable importance for secure Bayesian inference and data synthesis. If rigorously derived and validated, the explicit tCDP guarantees and utility analyses could be highly valuable for researchers and practitioners in privacy-preserving machine learning. However, given the substantial technical errors and clarity issues identified, the current results cannot be considered reliable or directly applicable without extensive revision and re-verification. While the paper offers updated calculations for a well-known idea, its current form, despite the valuable problem it addresses, makes its immediate suitability for NeurIPS questionable without significant corrections. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue if these flaws are addressed.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have commendably addressed both the technical limitations and potential negative societal impacts of their work in a separate section. They correctly highlight that large `ρ` values can lead to non-private sampling and stress the importance of transparency regarding prior parameters and privacy guarantees by deploying organizations. The discussion on the need for sufficient sample size for accuracy, especially in high-stakes decision-making, is crucial. Furthermore, the paper astutely points out the potential for disparate impact from uniform priors, where smaller populations may suffer from relatively larger statistical bias and less accurate private statistics. The suggestion to "privately impose larger prior parameters on larger populations" is a constructive idea for mitigating this issue. One additional point to consider is the practical complexity of determining optimal prior parameters (`α`) and the `γ` parameter in tCDP for real-world datasets, especially given the acknowledged divergence of the upper bound for large values of sample statistics. While theoretical minimization is presented, guidance on robust practical selection methods would further strengthen the work. This section is well-written and demonstrates a good awareness of ethical implications, assuming the technical claims were sound.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the inherent differential privacy properties of releasing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior distribution. The core contribution lies in the derivation of explicit and closed-form privacy guarantees using truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP) for Dirichlet posterior sampling, which are subsequently converted into approximate differential privacy guarantees. The authors meticulously analyze the utility of this privacy mechanism across two key applications: Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. For both scenarios, the study provides theoretical accuracy guarantees, quantifies required sample sizes, and offers a detailed comparative analysis with the Gaussian mechanism, highlighting the conditions under which each mechanism performs better.

**Main Review:**
The paper is well-organized and generally easy to follow, making a valuable contribution to the field of privacy-preserving data analysis. The related works are effectively summarized, enabling readers to contextualize the proposed methods against existing approaches. All notations and background definitions are clearly stated, providing a solid foundation for understanding the technical content.

**Originality:** The paper makes a significant and original contribution by providing explicit and closed-form tCDP guarantees specifically for Dirichlet posterior sampling. This builds upon existing, more general frameworks for exponential families but delivers concrete and highly practical bounds for a widely used distribution. The authors clearly delineate how their work differs from prior studies, such as those providing general frameworks without explicit forms or those focusing on data synthesis privacy. Their distinctive approach of incorporating prior parameters to the Dirichlet mechanism, contrasting with input space restrictions in other works, further demonstrates a novel methodology.

**Quality:** The submission is largely technically sound. The theoretical derivations for tCDP and approximate DP are well-presented, with a clear proof sketch for Theorem 1 and references to the appendix for full proofs. The mathematical tools, including polygamma functions, are appropriately applied. The utility analysis is comprehensive, offering theoretical bounds for KL-divergence and L-infinity error, and a thorough discussion of sample size requirements across different privacy regimes. The comparative analysis with the Gaussian mechanism is well-executed and provides practical insights, particularly regarding sparse data. The authors are commendably transparent about the limitations of their bounds and the trade-offs involved, suggesting a complete and honest evaluation.

While the mathematical derivations appear generally sound, some specific points require careful re-verification and refinement. One reviewer noted a potential inaccuracy regarding the monotonicity of $\psi'(x)$ in the proof sketch for Theorem 1 (L177), stating it is decreasing for $x > 0$ rather than increasing. This specific point warrants careful re-verification by the authors. Similarly, some approximations in the high- and low-privacy regimes (Sections 4.1 and 4.2) were flagged for potentially missing factors of 2 or $\sqrt{2}$. The notation for sensitivity, specifically $\Delta_2$ versus $\Delta_2^2$, also requires consistent application throughout the paper, and the adjacency definition for $x$ and $x'$ in Theorem 1 could benefit from explicit connection to underlying adjacent datasets for full clarity. These are important details that, once addressed, will strengthen the paper's technical rigor.

**Clarity:** The manuscript is generally clearly written and well-structured. The introduction effectively sets the stage, motivating the problem and outlining the paper's contributions in relation to existing literature. The background sections are concise and informative. The main results are presented logically, with accompanying discussions that enhance understanding. Figures are clear and effectively illustrate key concepts and comparisons, and the notation is consistent and well-defined, aiding readability.

However, the manuscript is unfortunately marred by numerous LaTeX artifacts (e.g., `(cid:81)`, `(cid:15)`) scattered throughout the text, including in equations and mathematical expressions, which significantly detract from readability and suggest a need for thorough proofreading. Minor clarity improvements could also be made, such as ensuring consistent formatting (e.g., between numbered and bulleted lists in the overview), clarifying figure labels (e.g., Figure 1), and refining mathematical descriptions (e.g., $\psi'(x)$ approximations in Section 2.4). The phrase "smaller (l_inf)-accuracy" in Section 4.2 (L280-281) was noted as potentially confusing or incorrectly used. Furthermore, the checklist response regarding complete proofs (2b) appears contradictory given the "short proof that skips some calculations" for Theorem 1 in the main text. Addressing these presentation issues will significantly enhance the manuscript's overall clarity and professionalism.

**Significance:** The results are highly significant for researchers and practitioners in privacy-preserving data analysis, especially those dealing with discrete data and Bayesian inference. Explicit and usable privacy guarantees for Dirichlet posterior sampling fill a critical gap, enabling more confident deployment of these techniques in sensitive contexts. The detailed utility analysis for Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histograms provides practical guidance for system design, including considerations for accuracy and sample size. The comparative insights between Dirichlet and Gaussian mechanisms are particularly useful for making informed choices about privacy mechanisms, advancing the state of the art in demonstrable ways. Despite the aforementioned points requiring technical and presentation refinement, the underlying problem addressed by the paper is of considerable importance, and the results, once rigorously verified, would be highly significant for secure Bayesian inference and data synthesis.

**Limitations And Societal Impact:**
The paper contains a commendable and thoughtful discussion on "Potential negative societal impacts," as well as technical limitations. The authors appropriately highlight that derived privacy bounds, while worst-case, can become loose when input $\xi$ values are large, affecting the practical strength of the guarantee. They correctly stress the danger of vacuously claiming privacy with unacceptably large $\rho$ values and emphasize the importance of transparency regarding prior parameters and privacy guarantees by deploying organizations. The discussion on the need for sufficient sample size for accuracy, especially in high-stakes decision-making, is crucial. Furthermore, the paper astutely points out the potential for disparate impact from uniform priors, where smaller populations may suffer from relatively larger statistical bias and less accurate private statistics. The suggestion to "privately impose larger prior parameters on larger populations" is a constructive idea for mitigating this issue, although the practical implications of "privately imposing" such parameters would require careful consideration to avoid introducing new privacy risks or biases. One additional point to consider is the practical complexity of determining optimal prior parameters ($\alpha$) and the $\gamma$ parameter in tCDP for real-world datasets, especially given the acknowledged divergence of the upper bound for large values of sample statistics. This section significantly strengthens the paper, demonstrating a responsible approach to the societal implications of privacy-preserving techniques.

**Rating:**
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.
reviews.main_review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.

Main Review:

In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
